events.description: On April 2, 1792, President Washington signed the Coinage Act, establishing the United States Mint in Philadelphia and creating the American monetary system based on the dollar as the standard unit of currency. The act established a bimetallic standard with fixed ratios between gold and silver coins, created denominations from the half-cent to the eagle ($10 gold coin), and defined the dollar as 371.25 grains of pure silver. The legislation, largely shaped by Alexander Hamilton's 1791 Report on the Establishment of a Mint, gave the young republic its own currency system independent of foreign coinage.
key_figures: David Rittenhouse (first Director of the Mint)

sources.name: Coinage Act of 1792, 1 Stat. 246 (full text in Statutes at Large)
Alexander Hamilton, Report on the Establishment of a Mint (1791)

events.certainty_note: Statutory text survives; date of enactment is definitive.

events.title: Claude Joseph Rouget de Lisle composes 'La Marseillaise'
events.description: On the night of April 25–26, 1792, Captain Claude Joseph Rouget de Lisle composed the 'Chant de guerre pour l'Armée du Rhin' (War Song for the Army of the Rhine) in Strasbourg, days after France declared war on Austria. The song was later popularized by volunteer battalions from Marseille marching to Paris in the summer of 1792, earning the name 'La Marseillaise.' It became the anthem of the Revolution and was adopted as the French national anthem in 1795. Its militant lyrics ('Aux armes, citoyens!') encapsulated the revolutionary spirit of popular mobilization against monarchical Europe.
key_figures: Claude Joseph Rouget de Lisle (army captain and composer)
Philippe-Frédéric de Dietrich (Mayor of Strasbourg, in whose home it was first performed)

sources.name: Rouget de Lisle's own accounts; contemporary Strasbourg municipal records
Michel Vovelle, 'La Marseillaise: War or Peace,' in Realms of Memory, ed. Pierre Nora (1996)

events.certainty_note: Date and circumstances of composition are well attested, though some details of the original evening performance may be embellished in later retellings.

events.title: Slave uprising and civil war in Saint-Domingue escalates
events.description: Throughout 1792, the massive slave insurrection that had erupted in August 1791 in the northern plain of Saint-Domingue continued and expanded. The colony descended into a multi-sided civil war involving enslaved Africans, free people of color, white planters, and competing colonial authorities. In April 1792, the French Legislative Assembly passed a decree granting full political rights to free men of color, and dispatched a civil commission led by Léger-Félicité Sonthonax to enforce it. Sonthonax arrived in September with 6,000 troops, but the situation remained chaotic. The uprising would ultimately lead to the abolition of slavery in the colony (1793) and Haitian independence (1804).

sources.name: French colonial correspondence and civil commission reports
C.L.R. James, The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (1938, rev. 1963)
Laurent Dubois, Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution (2004)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in French colonial archives, though enslaved people's perspectives are largely mediated through colonial records.

events.title: Mary Wollstonecraft publishes A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
events.description: In January 1792, Mary Wollstonecraft published A Vindication of the Rights of Woman, one of the earliest and most influential works of feminist philosophy. Writing in response to Talleyrand's report on French national education (which excluded women from civic education), Wollstonecraft argued that women were not naturally inferior to men but appeared so only because of lack of education. She called for women to be educated as rational beings and treated as equals in marriage and public life. The work was widely read and debated across Britain, France, and the United States, though it was later vilified after the posthumous publication of her personal life details by her husband William Godwin.
key_figures: Charles Maurice de Talleyrand-Périgord (French politician, whose education report provoked the work)

sources.name: Mary Wollstonecraft, A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792, first edition by J. Johnson)
Janet Todd, Mary Wollstonecraft: A Revolutionary Life (2000)

events.certainty_note: First edition survives; publication date and reception are well documented.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's court receives the planned Macartney Embassy
events.description: In 1792, the British government under Prime Minister William Pitt commissioned Lord Macartney to lead a diplomatic mission to the Qing court to open trade relations and establish a permanent embassy in Beijing. The embassy departed in September 1792 and would arrive in China in 1793. Meanwhile, in 1792 the Qianlong Emperor was focused on a military campaign against Nepalese (Gorkha) incursions into Tibet. Qing forces under Fukang'an successfully repelled the Gorkha invasion, forcing Nepal to accept tributary status. This campaign demonstrated the continued military reach of the Qing but also the rising costs of frontier defense.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty) for the Qianlong reign
James Hevia, Cherishing Men from Afar: Qing Guest Ritual and the Macartney Embassy of 1793 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Qing court records and British diplomatic archives both survive in detail.

events.title: Unzen volcanic eruption and tsunami kill thousands in Japan
events.description: On May 21, 1792, the collapse of the Maeyama dome of Mount Unzen in Kyushu triggered a massive landslide into Ariake Bay, generating a devastating tsunami. The combined disaster killed approximately 15,000 people, making it the deadliest volcanic event in Japanese history. The city of Shimabara and surrounding villages were devastated. The event is known in Japanese as the 'Shimabara Catastrophe' (島原大変). The disaster occurred during the Tokugawa shogunate's period of relative peace and internal stability, and the response tested the Edo-period administrative system's capacity for disaster relief.

sources.name: Contemporary Japanese records and domain (han) administrative documents
I. Inoue, 'The 1792 eruption of Unzen volcano and the Shimabara catastrophe,' in Volcanoes and History (2000)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in Tokugawa-era records; death toll estimates vary but the order of magnitude is consistent across sources.

events.title: Gustav III of Sweden assassinated at a masked ball
events.description: On March 16, 1792, King Gustav III of Sweden was shot at a masquerade ball at the Royal Opera House in Stockholm by Jacob Johan Anckarström, a former army officer acting in concert with a group of disaffected nobles. Gustav died of his wounds on March 29. The assassination was motivated by noble resentment of Gustav's autocratic reforms and his curtailment of aristocratic privileges. His death brought the regency of Duke Charles (later Charles XIII) for the young Gustav IV Adolf. The event later inspired Verdi's opera Un ballo in maschera (1859) and sent shockwaves through a Europe already alarmed by the French Revolution.
key_figures: Gustav IV Adolf (successor, minor at the time)

sources.name: Swedish court records and trial proceedings of Anckarström
H. Arnold Barton, Scandinavia in the Revolutionary Era, 1760–1815 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in Swedish legal and court records.

events.title: Zaman Shah takes the Afghan throne — Durrani succession struggle
events.description: In 1792, Zaman Shah Durrani seized the throne of the Durrani Empire from his brother Mahmud Shah, continuing the fratricidal succession struggles that had plagued the dynasty since the death of Ahmad Shah Durrani in 1772. Zaman Shah would rule until 1800, during which time he attempted to reassert Durrani authority over Punjab and contemplated invasions of India that alarmed the British East India Company. The chronic instability of the Durrani succession contributed to the fragmentation of Afghan central authority and the eventual rise of Dost Mohammad Khan and the Barakzai dynasty.
key_figures: Timur Shah Durrani (father, died 1793 — some sources place his death in 1793, making the succession struggle slightly later)

sources.name: Mountstuart Elphinstone, An Account of the Kingdom of Caubul (1815)
Christine Noelle, State and Tribe in Nineteenth-Century Afghanistan (1997)

events.certainty_note: Durrani succession dates are somewhat uncertain. Some historians place Timur Shah's death and Zaman Shah's accession in 1793 rather than 1792. The sequence of events is agreed upon; exact dating is ±1 year.

events.title: Captain George Vancouver begins survey of the Pacific Northwest coast
events.region: Pacific Northwest (modern British Columbia, Washington State)
events.description: In April 1792, Captain George Vancouver of the Royal Navy arrived on the Pacific Northwest coast aboard HMS Discovery, beginning a comprehensive survey that would last until 1794. Vancouver charted the coastline from California to Alaska with unprecedented precision, circumnavigated Vancouver Island, and established that no Northwest Passage existed at navigable latitudes. In the same waters, American captain Robert Gray discovered and entered the Columbia River on May 11, 1792 — a discovery that later served as a basis for American territorial claims to the Oregon Country. Vancouver's survey produced the definitive charts of the Northwest coast used for decades.
key_figures: Robert Gray (American merchant captain, discovered Columbia River)

sources.name: George Vancouver, A Voyage of Discovery to the North Pacific Ocean and Round the World (1798)
Robert Gray's ship logs (Columbia Rediviva)
Barry Gough, Fortune's a River: The Collision of Empires in Northwest America (2007)

events.certainty_note: Ship logs, charts, and published voyage accounts survive in detail.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War ends with the Treaty of Jassy
events.description: On January 9, 1792, the Treaty of Jassy (Iași) ended the Russo-Turkish War of 1787–1792. The treaty confirmed Russian annexation of the Crimea (seized in 1783) and extended the Russian frontier to the Dniester River, giving Russia control of the territory between the Bug and Dniester rivers (including the fortress of Ochakov). The Ottoman Empire ceded any claims to Georgia. While the territorial gains were modest compared to Russia's wartime ambitions, the treaty consolidated Russia's position as a Black Sea power and established the Dniester as the Russo-Ottoman border in the west. Catherine II's attention then shifted to Poland.
key_figures: Grigory Potemkin (Russian commander, died 1791 before treaty)

sources.name: Treaty of Jassy, January 9, 1792 (text in diplomatic archives)
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870: An Empire Besieged (2007)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and diplomatic correspondence survive in both Russian and Ottoman archives.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly repeated claims about 1792 events merit scrutiny: (1) Goethe's famous remark at Valmy ('From this place and from this day forth begins a new epoch in the history of the world') was not recorded until his 1822 memoir, thirty years later, and its authenticity is seriously doubted by scholars such as Johann Peter Eckermann's biographers; it may be a retrospective construction. (2) The military significance of Valmy itself is debated — some historians (notably Blanning) argue it was Brunswick's logistical crisis and dysentery in his army, not French military prowess, that caused the retreat. (3) The Buttonwood Agreement's narrative of founding the NYSE 'under a tree' contains elements of later mythologization; the agreement itself is real but the romantic origin story has accrued embellishments. (4) The dating of Zaman Shah Durrani's accession to 1792 vs. 1793 remains contested, as Timur Shah's death date is reported differently across sources. (5) Denmark's 1792 slave trade ban is sometimes cited as evidence of early progressive values, but the ten-year grace period actually led to a spike in slave imports, and the decree was motivated partly by mercantilist calculations about labor supply, not purely humanitarian sentiment.
historiographic_note: 1792 is richly documented from European and American perspectives, with abundant primary sources including legislative records, diplomatic correspondence, military dispatches, and published works. The year's documentation is heavily skewed toward the French Revolution, which dominates the historiography. Non-European events — particularly in Africa, Southeast Asia, the Pacific, and the interior Americas — are known primarily through European colonial records and are therefore filtered through European perspectives and priorities. The Haitian Revolution, despite its enormous significance, is documented primarily through French colonial archives, with the perspectives of enslaved people largely mediated through hostile observers. Japanese records of the Unzen disaster are reliable but less accessible in Western-language scholarship. Afghan and Central Asian chronology for this period relies on later compilations and is less precisely dated.

graph_edges.note: French declaration of war on Austria escalated into war with First Coalition including Britain, Netherlands, and Spain by 1793
Brunswick Manifesto directly provoked the August 10 insurrection against the monarchy
Fall of the Tuileries led directly to abolition of the monarchy and declaration of the Republic
Victory at Valmy gave the Convention confidence to proclaim the Republic the following day
Republic's establishment set the stage for Louis XVI's trial and execution in January 1793
French conquest of Belgium contributed to Britain's decision to enter the war in February 1793
Edict of Fraternity alarmed Britain and other neutral powers, contributing to expansion of the war
Treaty of Seringapatam's humiliation of Tipu Sultan set the stage for his final war against the British in 1799
Russian invasion of Poland led to the Second Partition of Poland in January 1793
1792 escalation of the Saint-Domingue uprising was part of the process that led to Haitian independence in 1804
Treaty of Jassy freed Russian military resources to focus on Poland
Macartney Embassy departed 1792, arrived at Qing court in 1793
Denmark's 1792 slave trade ban preceded and may have influenced the British abolition of the slave trade in 1807

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The year 1791 falls in the heart of the Age of Revolutions. The French Revolution, now in its third year, was radicalizing as the monarchy attempted to reassert control and failed. Across the Atlantic, the newly independent United States was consolidating its constitutional order with the Bill of Rights, while in the Caribbean, the Haitian Revolution erupted — the largest and most successful slave revolt in history. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor (who had abdicated in favor of Jiaqing in 1796, but this is 1791 so Qianlong still reigns) was at the tail end of its 'High Qing' prosperity, the Ottoman Empire was fighting a losing war against Russia, and European colonial powers continued to expand in India, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Aboriginal Australian communities
Pacific Island societies beyond early European contact
Central Asian steppe polities
Interior Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond Burmese and Siamese court records

events.title: Flight to Varennes
events.description: On 20–21 June 1791, King Louis XVI and the royal family attempted to flee Paris in disguise, aiming to reach the royalist fortress at Montmédy near the Austrian border. They were recognized and arrested at Varennes-en-Argonne. The failed escape devastated whatever remained of popular trust in the monarchy, radicalized republican sentiment, and made the fiction of a king cooperating willingly with the Revolution untenable. It accelerated the trajectory toward the abolition of the monarchy and the king's eventual trial and execution.
key_figures: Axel von Fersen (Swedish nobleman, organized the escape)
Jean-Baptiste Drouet (postmaster who recognized the king)

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts (e.g., L'Ami du peuple, Révolutions de Paris)
Proceedings of the National Assembly, June 1791
Timothy Tackett, When the King Took Flight (2003)

events.certainty_note: One of the most thoroughly documented episodes of the French Revolution, with abundant contemporary sources from multiple perspectives.

events.title: Champ de Mars massacre
events.description: On 17 July 1791, a crowd gathered at the Champ de Mars to sign a petition demanding the king's abdication following the Flight to Varennes. The National Guard under the Marquis de Lafayette opened fire on the demonstrators, killing dozens (estimates range from 12 to 50). The massacre marked a decisive split between moderate constitutional monarchists and radical republicans, drove the Cordeliers and Jacobin clubs underground temporarily, and foreshadowed the more radical phases of the Revolution.
key_figures: Marquis de Lafayette (commander of the National Guard)
Jean Sylvain Bailly (Mayor of Paris, who declared martial law)
Georges Danton (radical leader, fled Paris after the event)

sources.name: Procès-verbaux of the Paris Commune and National Assembly debates
Contemporary pamphlets and newspapers
David Andress, The Terror: Civil War in the French Revolution (2005)

events.certainty_note: Well documented, though casualty figures vary across sources.

events.title: French Constitution of 1791 adopted
events.description: On 3 September 1791, the National Constituent Assembly completed France's first written constitution, establishing a constitutional monarchy with a unicameral Legislative Assembly. The king accepted it on 14 September. The constitution enshrined civil liberties from the Declaration of the Rights of Man (1789), but restricted voting to 'active citizens' — men who paid a minimum level of taxation — excluding women, the poor, and servants. It was effectively dead on arrival: the Flight to Varennes had destroyed trust in the king, and the constitution would be superseded within a year by the declaration of a republic.
key_figures: Louis XVI (King of France, who accepted the constitution)
Honoré Gabriel Riqueti, comte de Mirabeau (influential deputy, died April 1791)

sources.name: Text of the Constitution of 1791 (Archives parlementaires)
Keith Michael Baker, Inventing the French Revolution (1990)

events.certainty_note: The constitutional text itself survives; its adoption and context are extensively documented.

events.title: Olympe de Gouges publishes Declaration of the Rights of Woman
events.description: In September 1791, playwright and political activist Olympe de Gouges published the Déclaration des droits de la femme et de la citoyenne (Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen), modeled on the 1789 Declaration of the Rights of Man. It argued that women were born free and equal to men, demanded female suffrage, and challenged the exclusion of women from citizenship rights in the new constitution. The text was largely ignored in its time but is now regarded as a foundational document in the history of feminism and human rights.

sources.name: Olympe de Gouges, Déclaration des droits de la femme et de la citoyenne (1791), original pamphlet
Joan Wallach Scott, Only Paradoxes to Offer: French Feminists and the Rights of Man (1996)

events.certainty_note: The text survives in its original published form.

events.title: Haitian Revolution begins (Bois Caïman ceremony and August uprising)
events.description: On the night of 14 August 1791, enslaved people gathered at Bois Caïman in the northern plain of Saint-Domingue for a Vodou ceremony that is traditionally regarded as the catalyst of the Haitian Revolution. On 22–23 August, a massive coordinated slave revolt erupted across the northern province, destroying sugar plantations and killing hundreds of enslavers. Within weeks, tens of thousands of enslaved people were in open rebellion under leaders including Boukman Dutty. This was the beginning of the only successful large-scale slave revolution in history, which would eventually lead to Haitian independence in 1804 and profoundly shake the Atlantic slave system.
key_figures: Boukman Dutty (early leader of the revolt, killed in November 1791)
Cécile Fatiman (Vodou priestess, traditionally associated with Bois Caïman)
Dutty Boukman's unnamed co-conspirators among enslaved plantation workers

sources.name: Antoine Dalmas, Histoire de la révolution de Saint-Domingue (1814) — earliest detailed account of the ceremony, written by a hostile white colonist
Colonial administrative correspondence and planter reports, August–September 1791 (Archives nationales d'outre-mer)
Laurent Dubois, Avengers of the New World: The Story of the Haitian Revolution (2004)

events.certainty_note: The August 1791 uprising is abundantly documented in colonial records. The Bois Caïman ceremony is traditional; its historicity is debated by scholars (see disconfirming evidence section), though the revolt itself is beyond question.

events.title: United States Bill of Rights ratified
events.description: On 15 December 1791, the first ten amendments to the United States Constitution — collectively known as the Bill of Rights — were ratified after Virginia became the eleventh state to approve them. The amendments guaranteed fundamental civil liberties including freedom of speech, religion, and the press; the right to bear arms; protections against unreasonable search and seizure; and due process rights. Originally proposed by James Madison in 1789 to address Anti-Federalist concerns about centralized power, these amendments became the bedrock of American constitutional law and jurisprudence.
key_figures: George Washington (President of the United States)
Thomas Jefferson (Secretary of State, advocate for a bill of rights)

sources.name: Original engrossed Bill of Rights manuscript (National Archives)
Congressional records and ratification documents of state legislatures
Akhil Reed Amar, The Bill of Rights: Creation and Reconstruction (1998)

events.certainty_note: Original documents survive in the National Archives; ratification dates are documented in state legislative records.

events.title: Canada Act (Constitutional Act) divides Quebec
events.description: On 10 June 1791, the British Parliament passed the Constitutional Act, which divided the Province of Quebec into Upper Canada (predominantly English-speaking, roughly modern Ontario) and Lower Canada (predominantly French-speaking, roughly modern Quebec). Each province received its own elected legislative assembly and appointed legislative council. The act was intended to manage tensions between Anglophone Loyalist settlers and the Francophone majority, but it created structural political conflicts — particularly in Lower Canada — that would fester for decades and contribute to the Rebellions of 1837–38.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
Guy Carleton, 1st Baron Dorchester (Governor of Quebec)

sources.name: Constitutional Act, 1791 (31 Geo. III, c. 31), Parliamentary records
Parliamentary debates (Hansard)
J.M. Bumsted, The Peoples of Canada: A Pre-Confederation History (2003)

events.certainty_note: Statutory text and parliamentary records survive.

events.title: Treaty of Sistova ends Austro-Turkish War
events.region: Ottoman Empire / Habsburg Empire (Sistova, modern Svishtov, Bulgaria)
events.description: On 4 August 1791, the Habsburg Empire and the Ottoman Empire signed the Treaty of Sistova, ending the Austro-Turkish War that had begun in 1788. Austria returned most of its conquests, including Belgrade, to the Ottomans, gaining only the town of Orsova and minor border adjustments. The treaty reflected Austria's need to redirect military resources toward the growing crisis with revolutionary France and the Polish situation. It marked the end of Habsburg expansionism at Ottoman expense in the Balkans for the time being.

sources.name: Treaty text in diplomatic archives (Haus-, Hof- und Staatsarchiv, Vienna)
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870: An Empire Besieged (2007)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and diplomatic correspondence survive in both Austrian and Ottoman archives.

events.title: Declaration of Pillnitz
events.description: On 27 August 1791, Emperor Leopold II and King Frederick William II of Prussia issued the Declaration of Pillnitz, stating that the situation of the French king was a matter of concern to all European sovereigns and hinting at military intervention to restore Louis XVI's authority. The declaration was deliberately vague — Leopold intended it as a bluff contingent on British support that he knew would not come — but it was read in France as a direct threat and inflamed revolutionary hostility toward foreign monarchies, contributing to the march toward war in 1792.
key_figures: Louis Auguste Le Tonnelier de Breteuil (royalist agent)

sources.name: Text of the Declaration of Pillnitz (diplomatic correspondence)
T.C.W. Blanning, The French Revolutionary Wars 1787–1802 (1996)

events.certainty_note: The declaration text survives and its context is well documented in diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Mozart dies in Vienna; completes The Magic Flute and Requiem left unfinished
events.description: Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart died on 5 December 1791 in Vienna at age 35 from a still-debated illness. In his final year he composed some of his greatest works: The Magic Flute (Die Zauberflöte), premiered on 30 September 1791 at the Theater auf der Wieden; La clemenza di Tito, composed for Leopold II's coronation as King of Bohemia; the Clarinet Concerto in A major; and the unfinished Requiem in D minor, later completed by his student Franz Xaver Süssmayr. His death left an enormous void in European musical life and generated enduring myths about poisoning and pauper's burial.
key_figures: Emanuel Schikaneder (librettist and impresario, The Magic Flute)
Franz Xaver Süssmayr (student, completed the Requiem)

sources.name: Mozart's personal correspondence and family letters (Mozart: Briefe und Aufzeichnungen, ed. Bauer and Deutsch)
Viennese death records and parish registers
Maynard Solomon, Mozart: A Life (1995)

events.certainty_note: Mozart's death date and final works are thoroughly documented. Cause of death remains medically debated.

events.title: Suppression of the Avignon Papacy and annexation to France
events.description: On 14 September 1791, the French National Assembly voted to annex the papal territories of Avignon and the Comtat Venaissin, which had been papal possessions since the 14th century. This followed a local insurrection and plebiscite in which inhabitants voted to join France. The annexation was one of several revolutionary challenges to papal authority and contributed to the deep rift between the Revolution and the Catholic Church. It also followed the violent Massacre of the Glacière in October 1791, when revolutionary partisans killed several dozen prisoners in Avignon's papal palace.
key_figures: Pope Pius VI (who protested the annexation)
Jourdan Coupe-Tête (leader of radical faction in Avignon)

sources.name: Decrees of the National Assembly, September 1791 (Archives parlementaires)
Michel Vovelle, The Revolution Against the Church: From Reason to the Supreme Being (1991)

events.certainty_note: Legislative records and local administrative documents survive.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's campaigns and British consolidation in Mysore
events.description: In 1791, the Third Anglo-Mysore War was underway, with British East India Company forces under Lord Cornwallis advancing on Tipu Sultan's capital at Seringapatam. Cornwallis captured the fortified position at Bangalore in March 1791 and laid siege to Seringapatam, though supply problems forced a retreat. Tipu Sultan, simultaneously facing Maratha and Hyderabadi attacks from the north and west as part of a triple alliance against him, was increasingly encircled. The war would end in 1792 with the Treaty of Seringapatam, stripping Tipu of half his territory.
key_figures: Charles Cornwallis (Governor-General of India and British commander)
Nizam Ali Khan (ruler of Hyderabad, allied with the British)

sources.name: East India Company military dispatches and Cornwallis correspondence (National Archives, UK)
Tipu Sultan's administrative records (partially surviving at the India Office Library)
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy (1997)

events.certainty_note: British military records are extensive; Tipu Sultan's own records partially survive.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaigns against Gurkhas in Tibet
events.description: In 1791, Gurkha forces from Nepal invaded Tibet for the second time, sacking the great monastery of Tashilhunpo and carrying off enormous wealth. The Qianlong Emperor dispatched a major Qing military expedition under Fuk'anggan to expel the Gurkhas and reassert Chinese suzerainty over Tibet. The Qing forces arrived in Tibet in late 1791 and would pursue the Gurkhas back into Nepal in 1792, ultimately forcing a Nepalese surrender. The campaign led to tightened Qing administrative control over Tibet, including the establishment of the Golden Urn lottery system for selecting reincarnated lamas.
key_figures: Shamarpa Lama (Tibetan religious figure who invited Gurkha intervention)

sources.name: Qing court records and Qianlong Emperor's edicts (Qing Shilu / Veritable Records)
Nepali court chronicles
Peter Schwieger, The Dalai Lama and the Emperor of China (2015)

events.certainty_note: Documented in both Qing imperial records and Nepali sources. The sack of Tashilhunpo is well attested.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War: Battle of Mačin
events.description: On 9 July 1791 (28 June Old Style), Russian forces under Prince Nikolai Repnin decisively defeated the Ottoman army at the Battle of Mačin (Măcin) on the Danube in what is now Romania. The Ottoman army suffered heavy casualties. This defeat, combined with the fall of the fortress of Anapa to Russian forces in June, compelled the Ottoman Empire to seek peace, leading to the Treaty of Jassy in January 1792. The war confirmed Russian control of the northern Black Sea coast and the annexation of Crimea.

sources.name: Russian military dispatches and Catherine II's correspondence
Ottoman chronicle accounts
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in both Russian and Ottoman military records.

events.title: Polish Constitution of 3 May (Konstytucja 3 maja)
events.description: On 3 May 1791, the Four-Year Sejm adopted the Constitution of 3 May, Europe's first modern codified national constitution and the world's second after the United States Constitution. It reformed the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth by abolishing the liberum veto (the requirement for unanimous consent that had paralyzed governance), established a constitutional monarchy with separation of powers, extended political rights to townspeople, and placed peasants under government protection. It was a dramatic attempt to save the Commonwealth from the predatory ambitions of Russia, Prussia, and Austria, but it provoked Catherine the Great's hostility and contributed directly to the Second Partition of Poland in 1793.
key_figures: Catherine the Great (Russian Empress, opposed the constitution)

sources.name: Text of the Constitution of 3 May 1791 (original in the Central Archives of Historical Records, Warsaw)
Proceedings of the Four-Year Sejm (Diariusz Sejmu)
Richard Butterwick, The Polish Revolution and the Catholic Church, 1788–1792 (2012)

events.certainty_note: The constitutional text survives in original form. Parliamentary proceedings are well documented.

events.title: Birmingham Riots (Priestley Riots)
events.description: On 14–17 July 1791, a 'Church and King' mob attacked the homes and laboratories of prominent Dissenters in Birmingham, most notably destroying the house and scientific laboratory of Joseph Priestley, the chemist who had co-discovered oxygen. The rioters were motivated by hostility toward Priestley's support for the French Revolution and religious dissent. Priestley fled to London and eventually emigrated to the United States. The riots reflected the deep polarization in British society over the French Revolution and demonstrated the volatility of radical-conservative tensions.
key_figures: Edmund Burke (whose Reflections on the Revolution in France had inflamed anti-radical sentiment)

sources.name: Joseph Priestley, An Appeal to the Public on the Subject of the Riots in Birmingham (1791)
Home Office correspondence and trial records (The National Archives, Kew)
Jenny Uglow, The Lunar Men: Five Friends Whose Curiosity Changed the World (2002)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary accounts, legal records, and Priestley's own writings.
cross_references: 1790-Burke's Reflections on the Revolution in France

events.title: Toussaint Louverture joins the Haitian Revolution
events.description: In the months following the August 1791 slave uprising, Toussaint Louverture — a formerly enslaved man who had been freed for about 15 years and was literate and knowledgeable about military strategy — joined the rebel forces. Initially serving under early leaders like Jean-François and Biassou, Toussaint quickly distinguished himself through military skill and political acumen. He would eventually become the dominant leader of the revolution, transforming an insurgency into a disciplined military and political movement. His entry into the conflict in late 1791 marked the beginning of one of the most remarkable military careers of the era.

sources.name: Colonial correspondence referencing early rebel leadership (Archives nationales d'outre-mer)
C.L.R. James, The Black Jacobins: Toussaint L'Ouverture and the San Domingo Revolution (1938; revised 1963)
Philippe Girard, Toussaint Louverture: A Revolutionary Life (2016)

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Toussaint's entry into the revolt is uncertain; sources differ on whether he joined in September, October, or November 1791. That he joined in late 1791 is the scholarly consensus.

events.title: Vermont admitted as the fourteenth US state
events.description: On 4 March 1791, Vermont was admitted to the Union as the fourteenth state and the first to join after the original thirteen. Vermont had functioned as an independent republic since 1777 and was the first American jurisdiction to partially abolish adult slavery in its constitution. Its admission resolved a long-standing territorial dispute with New York and was paired with Kentucky's admission (1792) to maintain the balance between northern and southern states.

sources.name: Act of Congress admitting Vermont, 18 February 1791
Michael Bellesiles, Revolutionary Outlaws: Ethan Allen and the Struggle for Independence on the Early American Frontier (1993)

events.certainty_note: Federal legislative records confirm the date.

events.title: Selim III begins Ottoman military reforms (Nizam-ı Cedid)
events.description: Sultan Selim III, confronting Ottoman military defeats against Russia and Austria, began implementing the Nizam-ı Cedid ('New Order') reforms in 1791–1792. These reforms aimed to create a modern European-style army trained by foreign (primarily French) advisors, alongside fiscal reforms to fund the new military. The Nizam-ı Cedid represented the most ambitious Ottoman modernization effort to date, but faced fierce resistance from the Janissary corps and conservative ulema. The program would ultimately contribute to Selim's deposition and murder in 1807–1808.
key_figures: Ebubekir Ratip Efendi (Ottoman ambassador whose reports on European armies influenced reform)

sources.name: Ottoman administrative records and Selim III's reform memoranda (hatt-ı hümayun)
Stanford J. Shaw, Between Old and New: The Ottoman Empire Under Sultan Selim III, 1789–1807 (1971)

events.certainty_note: Ottoman archival sources document the reform program, though the exact start date is gradual rather than a single event.

events.title: Luigi Galvani publishes De viribus electricitatis
events.description: In 1791, Italian physician Luigi Galvani published De viribus electricitatis in motu musculari commentarius (Commentary on the Effect of Electricity on Muscular Motion), describing his experiments showing that frog legs twitched when touched with two different metals. Galvani attributed this to 'animal electricity' inherent in living tissue. While his theoretical explanation was contested by Alessandro Volta — who argued the electricity came from the contact between dissimilar metals, leading to the invention of the battery — Galvani's work founded the field of bioelectricity and electrophysiology and profoundly influenced both science and culture (inspiring, among other things, Mary Shelley's Frankenstein).
key_figures: Alessandro Volta (physicist, who would dispute Galvani's interpretation)

sources.name: Luigi Galvani, De viribus electricitatis in motu musculari commentarius (1791), published in the proceedings of the Bologna Academy of Sciences
Marco Bresadola, 'Medicine and Science in the Life of Luigi Galvani', Brain Research Bulletin 46 (1998)

events.certainty_note: The original publication survives and is dated precisely.

events.title: French Le Chapelier Law bans trade guilds and workers' associations
events.description: On 14 June 1791, the National Assembly passed the Le Chapelier Law, which banned guilds, trade unions, and all forms of workers' associations, including the right to strike. Framed in the language of individual liberty and free enterprise, the law reflected the Revolution's hostility to corporate intermediary bodies between the citizen and the state. It effectively prohibited collective labor action in France for nearly a century — the law was not fully repealed until 1884. It demonstrated the bourgeois character of the early Revolution's economic liberalism.
key_figures: Isaac René Guy le Chapelier (deputy and sponsor of the law)

sources.name: Text of the Le Chapelier Law (Archives parlementaires, 14 June 1791)
William Sewell, Work and Revolution in France: The Language of Labor from the Old Regime to 1848 (1980)

events.certainty_note: Legislative text and parliamentary debates survive.

events.title: George Vancouver expedition departs for the Pacific Northwest
events.description: On 1 April 1791, Captain George Vancouver departed Falmouth, England, commanding HMS Discovery and HMS Chatham on a major expedition to survey the Pacific Northwest coast of North America. The expedition's goals included receiving the territory at Nootka Sound from Spain under the Nootka Convention, surveying the coastline from California to Alaska to determine whether a Northwest Passage existed, and charting the coast in detail. The voyage lasted until 1795 and produced some of the most detailed maritime surveys of the era, establishing British claims in the region.
key_figures: George Vancouver (Royal Navy captain and expedition leader)

sources.name: George Vancouver, A Voyage of Discovery to the North Pacific Ocean and Round the World (1798)
Admiralty records and ship logs (The National Archives, Kew)

events.certainty_note: Vancouver's own published account and Admiralty records provide precise dates.

events.title: Slave revolt in Dominica (Colihaut uprising)
events.description: In early 1791, enslaved people in Dominica staged an uprising influenced by the revolutionary ferment in neighboring French colonies. The revolt, centered in the Colihaut area, was part of a broader pattern of Caribbean slave resistance emboldened by the ideals of the French Revolution and news of the free people of color's political mobilization in Saint-Domingue. While suppressed by British forces, the uprising was one of several that demonstrated the interconnected nature of revolutionary movements across the Caribbean in this period.

sources.name: Colonial Office correspondence (CO 71, The National Archives, Kew)
David Barry Gaspar and David Patrick Geggus, eds., A Turbulent Time: The French Revolution and the Greater Caribbean (1997)

events.certainty_note: Colonial administrative records document the uprising, though details are sparse compared to larger revolts.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above have contested elements: (1) The Bois Caïman ceremony (1791-005): Historian David Geggus has argued that the ceremony's details — particularly the Vodou ritual elements — may be largely legendary, drawn from Antoine Dalmas's hostile 1814 account rather than contemporary evidence. The ceremony has become a foundational national myth for Haiti, but its specific details (the pig sacrifice, the oath) are not attested in any contemporaneous source. The uprising itself is unquestioned; the ceremony's precise nature is not. (2) Mozart's cause of death (1791-010): Over 100 causes of death have been proposed, ranging from rheumatic fever to mercury poisoning to chronic kidney disease. The persistent legend that Antonio Salieri poisoned Mozart has no credible evidence and was already debunked in the 19th century, though it persists in popular culture via Pushkin and the film Amadeus. (3) The Polish Constitution of 3 May (1791-015): Its adoption was partly achieved by political maneuvering — reformers deliberately chose a date when many conservative opponents were still away from Warsaw after Easter recess. Some historians note this raises questions about its democratic legitimacy, though it was subsequently affirmed by a fuller session. (4) Toussaint Louverture's early role (1791-017): Some scholars have questioned whether Toussaint initially helped protect his former master's property before joining the revolt, complicating the narrative of him as an immediate revolutionary. Philippe Girard's 2016 biography presents evidence that Toussaint may have been more cautious and calculating in his early involvement than the heroic narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: 1791 is exceptionally well documented. The French Revolution generated an enormous volume of printed material — newspapers, pamphlets, legislative records, correspondence — making it one of the most densely sourced periods in history. American constitutional records are similarly abundant. The Haitian Revolution, however, suffers from significant source biases: most contemporary accounts were written by white colonists or French officials hostile to the enslaved population, and the voices of the enslaved themselves survive only through mediated accounts. The Qing and Ottoman sources exist in abundance in their respective archives but are underrepresented in English-language historiography. South Asian sources for the Anglo-Mysore War are heavily tilted toward British East India Company records; Tipu Sultan's own administrative records survive only partially. African and Pacific history for this period remains poorly represented in the available sources, reflecting both archival destruction and the biases of colonial-era scholarship.

graph_edges.note: The Flight to Varennes destroyed public trust in the monarchy and accelerated the move toward republicanism
The August 1791 slave uprising was the opening act of the Haitian Revolution, which culminated in independence in 1804
The Bill of Rights was demanded as a condition for ratification of the 1787 Constitution
The Declaration of Pillnitz inflamed French revolutionary hostility and contributed to the declaration of war in April 1792
The constitution provoked Russian and Prussian hostility, directly leading to military intervention and the second partition
The 1791 campaigns set the stage for Tipu Sultan's defeat and the harsh peace of 1792
The Russian victory at Mačin forced the Ottoman Empire to accept the Treaty of Jassy
The Qing expedition dispatched in 1791 pursued the Gurkhas into Nepal in 1792
Galvani's experiments and Volta's reinterpretation of them led directly to the invention of the voltaic pile
Gouges published her Declaration in direct response to the exclusion of women from the new constitution
The August uprising drew Toussaint Louverture into the revolutionary movement
The Flight to Varennes prompted the republican petition that led to the Champ de Mars massacre
Louis XVI's failed flight alarmed European monarchs and prompted the Declaration of Pillnitz

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The year 1790 sits at the dawn of the Age of Revolutions. The French Revolution, entering its second year, was transforming France from an absolute monarchy into a constitutional state while its leaders debated how far reforms should go. Across the Atlantic, the United States was consolidating its new federal government under President George Washington, with the capital moving from New York to Philadelphia. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most populous state, though internal pressures were building. The Ottoman Empire was engaged in war with Austria and Russia, the British were expanding their control in India, and colonial empires in the Americas continued to exploit enslaved labor on a massive scale. The Industrial Revolution was accelerating in Britain, with textile mills and steam power reshaping economic life.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading states
Aboriginal Australian communities
Pacific Island societies beyond initial European contact zones
Central Asian steppe polities
Interior South American indigenous societies

events.title: Civil Constitution of the Clergy
events.description: On 12 July 1790, the National Constituent Assembly passed the Civil Constitution of the Clergy, which subordinated the Catholic Church in France to the state. Bishops and priests were to be elected by their districts and paid by the government, and papal authority over the French church was effectively abolished. The law provoked a profound schism: when a loyalty oath was required in November, roughly half the clergy refused. This split French society between 'constitutional' and 'refractory' clergy, alienating devout rural populations and creating one of the most enduring and divisive legacies of the Revolution.
key_figures: Armand-Gaston Camus (chairman of the ecclesiastical committee)
Pope Pius VI (condemned the constitution in 1791)

sources.name: Archives parlementaires de 1787 à 1860, Series 1
Timothy Tackett, Religion, Revolution, and Regional Culture in Eighteenth-Century France (1986)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in legislative records, contemporary pamphlets, and clerical correspondence.

events.title: Fête de la Fédération
events.description: On 14 July 1790, the first anniversary of the storming of the Bastille, a massive ceremony was held at the Champ de Mars in Paris. An estimated 300,000 spectators watched as King Louis XVI swore an oath to the nation and the law, and the Marquis de Lafayette led a mass oath of loyalty from National Guard federations across France. The event was meant to symbolize national unity and the reconciliation of monarchy with revolution. In retrospect, it marked the high-water mark of revolutionary optimism before the deepening divisions of 1791–1792.
key_figures: Marquis de Lafayette (commander of the National Guard)
Charles Maurice de Talleyrand (Bishop of Autun, celebrated the mass)

sources.name: Contemporary press accounts and official proceedings of the fête
Mona Ozouf, Festivals and the French Revolution (1988)

events.certainty_note: One of the most widely witnessed and documented events of the early Revolution.

events.title: Abolition of the nobility in France
events.description: On 19 June 1790, the National Constituent Assembly abolished hereditary nobility, coats of arms, titles, and feudal privileges. All citizens were henceforth to be legally equal. This decree dismantled the juridical basis of the ancien régime's social hierarchy, though enforcement was uneven and many former nobles retained economic power. The abolition drove some aristocrats into emigration and foreign courts, contributing to the émigré crisis and eventual counter-revolutionary military threats.
key_figures: Philippe-Antoine Merlin de Douai (jurist and deputy)
Charles de Lameth (reformist noble who supported abolition)

sources.name: Decree of 19 June 1790, Archives parlementaires
William Doyle, The Oxford History of the French Revolution (2002)

events.certainty_note: Legislative record is definitive.

events.title: Residence Act establishes a new U.S. capital on the Potomac
events.description: On 16 July 1790, the Residence Act authorized the creation of a permanent national capital on the Potomac River, in a district not exceeding ten miles square. This was the result of the famous 'Dinner Table Bargain' between Alexander Hamilton, Thomas Jefferson, and James Madison, in which Southern support for Hamilton's financial plan (federal assumption of state debts) was traded for a Southern location for the capital. Philadelphia would serve as the temporary capital for ten years while the new city — later named Washington — was constructed.

sources.name: Residence Act (1 Stat. 130), 16 July 1790
Jefferson's account of the 'Dinner Table Bargain' (written later, c. 1818)
Jacob E. Cooke, 'The Compromise of 1790,' William and Mary Quarterly (1970)

events.certainty_note: The Residence Act is a statutory record. The exact nature of the behind-the-scenes bargain is debated, with Jefferson's later account being the primary (and possibly self-serving) source.

events.title: Funding Act: Federal assumption of state debts
events.description: On 4 August 1790, Congress passed the Funding Act, which authorized the federal government to assume the remaining war debts of the states from the American Revolution — approximately $21.5 million. Championed by Treasury Secretary Alexander Hamilton, this was a cornerstone of his financial program to establish federal creditworthiness and bind state economic interests to the national government. It was deeply controversial: states like Virginia that had already paid their debts objected to subsidizing those that had not. The compromise that secured passage was linked to the Residence Act.

sources.name: An Act making provision for the Debt of the United States (1 Stat. 138), 4 August 1790
Ron Chernow, Alexander Hamilton (2004)

events.certainty_note: Statutory and Congressional record is definitive.
cross_references: 1791-Establishment of the First Bank of the United States

events.title: First U.S. Census conducted
events.description: Beginning on 2 August 1790 and taking approximately 18 months to complete, the first United States Census enumerated the population at 3,929,214 persons. This included approximately 694,280 enslaved people, counted as three-fifths of a person for apportionment purposes under the Constitution's compromise clause. The census was constitutionally mandated for congressional apportionment. Virginia was the most populous state, and the data shaped the first reapportionment of the House of Representatives.
key_figures: Thomas Jefferson (Secretary of State, oversaw the census)

sources.name: Return of the Whole Number of Persons within the Several Districts of the United States (1791, official census publication)
Margo J. Anderson, The American Census: A Social History (1988)

events.certainty_note: Official government records; minor undercounting acknowledged by contemporaries, but the event itself is indisputable.

events.title: Treaty of Värälä ends Russo-Swedish War
events.description: On 14 August 1790, the Treaty of Värälä ended the Russo-Swedish War (1788–1790) with no territorial changes. The war had been initiated by Gustav III of Sweden, who hoped to exploit Russia's simultaneous conflict with the Ottoman Empire. Despite some Swedish naval victories — notably the Second Battle of Svensksund (9 July 1790), one of the largest naval battles in history by number of vessels engaged — Sweden lacked the resources to press its advantage. The status quo ante bellum peace freed Russia to concentrate on its Ottoman war.

sources.name: Treaty text in C. F. von Martens, Recueil des traités
R. C. Anderson, Naval Wars in the Baltic (1969)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and diplomatic correspondence survive in both Swedish and Russian archives.

events.title: Second Battle of Svensksund
events.description: On 9–10 July 1790, the Swedish navy under Gustav III's personal command decisively defeated the Russian Baltic fleet at Svensksund (modern Ruotsinsalmi, Finland). The Swedes destroyed or captured approximately one-third of the Russian fleet and inflicted some 9,000 casualties. By number of combatant vessels (nearly 500 ships total), it was among the largest naval engagements in history. The victory strengthened Sweden's hand in the subsequent Treaty of Värälä negotiations, allowing Gustav III to end the war without territorial losses.
key_figures: Gustav III (King of Sweden, commanded personally)
Charles Henry of Nassau-Siegen (Russian fleet commander)

sources.name: Swedish and Russian official battle reports
Jan Glete, Navies and Nations: Warships, Navies and State Building in Europe and America, 1500–1860 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented naval engagement with reports from both sides. Casualty figures vary by source but the scale and outcome are not disputed.

events.title: Convention of Reichenbach
events.description: On 27 July 1790, the Convention of Reichenbach was concluded between Austria (under the new Emperor Leopold II) and Prussia, mediated by Britain and the Dutch Republic. Austria agreed to end its war against the Ottoman Empire on the basis of the status quo ante bellum, abandoning most of the conquests made under Joseph II. This diplomatic retreat defused the threat of a Prussian attack on Austria and allowed Leopold II to focus on the gathering domestic crises in his Habsburg domains, including unrest in the Austrian Netherlands and Hungary.
key_figures: Leopold II (Holy Roman Emperor, succeeded Joseph II in February 1790)

sources.name: Convention text and diplomatic correspondence in Austrian and Prussian state archives
T. C. W. Blanning, Joseph II (1994)

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic record is complete and well-preserved.

events.title: Leopold II succeeds Joseph II as Holy Roman Emperor
events.description: On 20 February 1790, Joseph II died after a decade of ambitious but polarizing reforms. His brother Leopold II, previously Grand Duke of Tuscany, succeeded him. Leopold inherited multiple crises: rebellion in the Austrian Netherlands (Belgian provinces), unrest in Hungary, and an expensive war with the Ottoman Empire. A skilled diplomat and pragmatist, Leopold reversed many of Joseph's most controversial reforms, pacified Hungary by restoring its traditional constitution, and negotiated peace with the Ottomans and Prussia, stabilizing the Habsburg state — at least temporarily.

sources.name: Court records and contemporary correspondence of the Habsburg dynasty
Adam Wandruszka, Leopold II (1963–1965)

events.certainty_note: Death and succession dates are definitively documented.

events.title: Nootka Crisis between Britain and Spain
events.description: In 1790, the Nootka Crisis brought Britain and Spain to the brink of war over competing claims to the Pacific Northwest coast of North America. Spanish forces had seized British trading vessels and a small settlement at Nootka Sound (on Vancouver Island) in 1789. Britain, under Prime Minister William Pitt, mobilized its fleet and demanded restitution. Spain, unable to secure French alliance support (France was consumed by revolution), backed down. The Nootka Convention of October 1790 forced Spain to restore the seized property and effectively ended Spain's claim to exclusive sovereignty over the Pacific Northwest, opening the region to British and later American expansion.
key_figures: William Pitt the Younger (British Prime Minister)
Esteban José Martínez (Spanish naval officer who seized the ships)

sources.name: Nootka Convention (28 October 1790), treaty text
Warren L. Cook, Flood Tide of Empire: Spain and the Pacific Northwest, 1543–1819 (1973)

events.certainty_note: Treaty and diplomatic correspondence well-preserved in British and Spanish archives.

events.title: Tipu Sultan concludes Treaty of Srirangapatna
events.description: In March 1790, the Third Anglo-Mysore War (1789–1792) was escalating. Tipu Sultan of Mysore had invaded the territories of the British East India Company's ally, the Raja of Travancore, in late 1789, prompting Governor-General Cornwallis to form an alliance with the Nizam of Hyderabad and the Maratha Confederacy against Mysore. The year 1790 saw British and allied forces campaign in Mysore territory, with mixed results. The war would continue until 1792 and result in significant territorial losses for Tipu. The conflict was part of the broader British consolidation of power in the Indian subcontinent.

sources.name: East India Company records and Cornwallis's dispatches
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy (1997)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in British East India Company records and Mysorean court records.

events.title: Mutiny on the Bounty aftermath: Fletcher Christian settles Pitcairn Island
events.description: In January 1790, the Bounty mutineers under Fletcher Christian, along with Tahitian men and women, arrived at uninhabited Pitcairn Island and burned the Bounty to avoid detection. This followed the famous mutiny of April 1789. The small settlement would remain unknown to the outside world until 1808. The Pitcairn community, born from an act of naval mutiny, developed into one of the most isolated human settlements on Earth. Its troubled early years — marked by violence, murder, and the deaths of most of the original settlers — were only later revealed.

sources.name: Account of John Adams (sole surviving mutineer), recorded by visiting ships from 1808 onward
Caroline Alexander, The Bounty: The True Story of the Mutiny on the Bounty (2003)

events.certainty_note: The arrival date is reconstructed from later testimony of the sole surviving mutineer, John Adams. No contemporary written records were made on Pitcairn.

events.title: First U.S. patent and copyright laws enacted
events.description: On 10 April 1790, President Washington signed the Patent Act, creating the first patent system in the United States. On 31 May 1790, the Copyright Act followed, granting authors 14-year copyright terms. The Patent Act established a board of three cabinet members (including Jefferson and Hamilton) to review applications. The first U.S. patent was granted on 31 July 1790 to Samuel Hopkins for a process of making potash. These laws implemented the Constitution's intellectual property clause and established frameworks that would shape American innovation policy.
key_figures: Thomas Jefferson (served on the patent board)

sources.name: Patent Act of 1790 (1 Stat. 109); Copyright Act of 1790 (1 Stat. 124)
B. Zorina Khan, The Democratization of Invention (2005)

events.certainty_note: Statutory record is definitive.

events.title: Benjamin Franklin dies
events.description: On 17 April 1790, Benjamin Franklin died in Philadelphia at the age of 84. One of the most celebrated figures of the Enlightenment, Franklin had been a printer, scientist, diplomat, inventor, and statesman. His funeral drew approximately 20,000 mourners — perhaps the largest gathering in American history to that point. The French National Assembly declared three days of mourning, a remarkable tribute reflecting Franklin's role as ambassador to France and his symbolic importance to the revolutionary cause. His will included bequests to Boston and Philadelphia that demonstrated his characteristic blend of pragmatism and civic vision.

sources.name: Contemporary obituaries and funeral accounts in Philadelphia and European newspapers
Walter Isaacson, Benjamin Franklin: An American Life (2003)

events.certainty_note: Death date and circumstances extensively documented.

events.title: Establishment of the U.S. Supreme Court
events.description: On 2 February 1790, the U.S. Supreme Court held its first session at the Royal Exchange building in New York City, with Chief Justice John Jay presiding and only three of six justices present. The Court had no cases on its docket and adjourned after organizational business. Though inauspicious at the time, this institutional founding would grow into one of the most powerful judicial bodies in history. The Court's early years were marked by difficulty attracting and retaining justices, and its authority would not be firmly established until Marbury v. Madison in 1803.

sources.name: Minutes of the Supreme Court of the United States, February 1790
Bernard Schwartz, A History of the Supreme Court (1993)

events.certainty_note: Court records are definitive.

events.title: France reorganized into départements
events.description: On 26 February 1790, the National Constituent Assembly formally divided France into 83 départements, replacing the old provinces, governorships, and patchwork of historical jurisdictions of the ancien régime. The départements were designed to be roughly equal in size (a day's horse ride from the capital to any border) and to break the power of provincial identities and aristocratic patronage networks. This rationalist administrative reorganization proved remarkably durable — the départemental system remains the foundation of French local government today, with only minor modifications.
key_figures: Emmanuel Joseph Sieyès (influential in constitutional design)
Jacques Guillaume Thouret (presented the plan to the Assembly)

sources.name: Decree of 26 February 1790, Archives parlementaires
Marie-Vic Ozouf-Marignier, La formation des départements (1989)

events.certainty_note: Legislative record is definitive and the institutional result is still visible today.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor campaigns against Gurkha incursions into Tibet
events.description: In 1790, tensions escalated between the Qing Empire and the Gurkha Kingdom of Nepal over trade disputes and Nepalese raids into Tibet. Gurkha forces had raided Tibetan monasteries, prompting the Qianlong Emperor to prepare a major military expedition. The full-scale Qing campaign would be launched in 1791–1792, eventually driving the Gurkhas back and forcing Nepal to accept tributary status. This episode demonstrated the Qing dynasty's continued willingness to project military power into the Himalayan frontier, though at enormous cost.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Max Oidtmann, Forging the Golden Urn: The Qing Empire and the Politics of Reincarnation in Tibet (2018)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Qing imperial records. The 1790 escalation phase is less documented than the 1791–1792 campaign itself.

events.title: Slave trade debates intensify in the British Parliament
events.description: In 1790, William Wilberforce continued his parliamentary campaign for the abolition of the slave trade, which he had first formally introduced in 1789. A select committee heard extensive testimony from both abolitionists and slave-trade defenders. The House of Commons voted to hear further evidence rather than act, effectively delaying abolition. The campaign mobilized unprecedented public petition drives, consumer boycotts of slave-produced sugar, and the testimony of formerly enslaved people like Olaudah Equiano (whose autobiography had been published in 1789). Full abolition of the British slave trade would not come until 1807.
key_figures: Olaudah Equiano (formerly enslaved author and activist)

sources.name: Hansard parliamentary debates and Select Committee on the Slave Trade reports
Adam Hochschild, Bury the Chains: Prophets and Rebels in the Fight to Free an Empire's Slaves (2005)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary record is complete.

events.title: Maratha-Mysore tensions and Nizam alliance shifts in India
events.description: In 1790, the Maratha Confederacy formally joined the British-Nizam alliance against Tipu Sultan of Mysore, creating a powerful triple alliance. The Marathas, under the Peshwa's nominal authority but effectively controlled by Nana Fadnavis, saw an opportunity to contain Mysorean power and expand their own southern territories. This alliance reshaped the power dynamics of the Indian subcontinent, isolating Tipu Sultan and laying the groundwork for the eventual British dominance of southern India. The war demonstrated how Indian powers' mutual rivalries facilitated British expansion.

sources.name: East India Company records and Maratha diplomatic correspondence (Peshwe Daftar)
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in both British and Indian archival sources.

events.title: Polish reform movement and preparations for the May 3 Constitution
events.description: Throughout 1790, the Four-Year Sejm (1788–1792) continued its ambitious reform program for the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. The Sejm passed the 'Towns Act' on 18 April 1791 granting rights to burghers, but preparations in 1790 were critical: alliances were sought with Prussia (which would prove treacherous), and reformers including Hugo Kołłątaj and Ignacy Potocki drafted constitutional proposals. The alliance with Prussia (signed March 1790) was intended to protect Poland from Russian interference during reforms but would prove hollow. These efforts culminated in the Constitution of 3 May 1791, Europe's first modern written constitution.

sources.name: Sejm records and contemporary political pamphlets
Jerzy Łukowski, Liberty's Folly: The Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth in the Eighteenth Century (1991)

events.certainty_note: Legislative and diplomatic records survive in Polish and Prussian archives.

events.title: Austrian Netherlands revolt collapses
events.description: The United Belgian States, proclaimed in January 1790 after a successful revolt against Habsburg rule, quickly disintegrated due to internal divisions between conservative Statists (led by Henri van der Noot) and progressive Vonckists. Leopold II, having succeeded Joseph II in February, adopted a conciliatory approach, promising to respect traditional privileges. By December 1790, Austrian troops reoccupied Brussels with minimal resistance. The brief independence movement demonstrated both the depth of resentment against Joseph II's centralizing reforms and the inability of the Belgian provinces to maintain unity without external support.

sources.name: Proclamation of the United Belgian States and contemporary political pamphlets
Janet Polasky, Revolution in Brussels 1787–1793 (1987)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in contemporary records and extensive modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The 'Dinner Table Bargain' (events 1790-004 and 1790-005) is based primarily on Thomas Jefferson's account written approximately 28 years after the fact (c. 1818). Historian Kenneth Bowling and others have argued that the compromise was more complex than a single dinner conversation, involving multiple negotiations over weeks. Jefferson's account may simplify and self-aggrandize his role. The narrative of a neat quid pro quo between assumption and the capital location is likely an oversimplification of a messier political process. Additionally, the Fête de la Fédération (1790-002) is often romanticized as a moment of genuine national unity, but scholars like Mona Ozouf have shown that significant coercion, orchestrated spectacle, and regional reluctance lay behind the apparently spontaneous celebrations. The attendance figure of 300,000 is an estimate from enthusiastic contemporaries and may be inflated. Regarding the Nootka Crisis (1790-011), some scholars argue Spain's capitulation was less about French revolutionary distraction and more about fundamental Spanish naval weakness relative to Britain, which had been growing for decades.
historiographic_note: 1790 is extremely well-documented in European and American archives, with extensive legislative records, diplomatic correspondence, newspapers, and private letters surviving from France, Britain, the United States, and the Habsburg and Russian empires. The record is overwhelmingly Eurocentric and elite-focused: the experiences of enslaved people, peasants, indigenous populations, and women are recoverable only through the distorting lens of literate male observers. For South and East Asia, court records from the Qing, Mughal successor states, and colonial trading companies provide substantial documentation, though often filtered through imperial or commercial perspectives. Sub-Saharan Africa, interior Southeast Asia, Aboriginal Australia, and Oceania remain largely undocumented by written sources for this period, though oral traditions and archaeological evidence provide some information.

graph_edges.note: The Civil Constitution provoked Pope Pius VI's formal condemnation of the Revolution in 1791
The unity proclaimed at the Fête was shattered by the Flight to Varennes one year later
The Residence Act and Funding Act were linked through the Dinner Table Bargain compromise
Federal debt assumption was the first step in Hamilton's financial program; the Bank was the second
Gustav III's autocratic warmaking contributed to the aristocratic conspiracy that assassinated him in 1792
The Convention of Reichenbach set the terms that led to the formal Austrian-Ottoman peace at Sistova
The Nootka Convention opened the Pacific Northwest to British exploration, leading to Vancouver's survey
The 1790 campaigns were part of the Third Anglo-Mysore War that concluded with the 1792 treaty
The départemental reorganization was a logical extension of the destruction of feudal jurisdictions in August 1789
The 1790 parliamentary debates were one phase of the long campaign that culminated in the 1807 Abolition Act
The 1790 reform preparations and Prussian alliance directly enabled the 1791 constitution
Leopold II's conciliatory approach to the Austrian Netherlands was enabled by his accession and reversal of Joseph II's policies

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The late eighteenth century was defined by the collision of Enlightenment political philosophy with ancien régime structures across Europe, the consolidation of the newly independent United States, the height of the Atlantic slave trade, and the continuing expansion of European colonial empires in Asia and the Pacific. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor was in its final prosperous decades, the Ottoman Empire faced reform pressures, and the Mughal Empire had effectively fragmented into successor states. 1789 stands as one of the most consequential single years in recorded history, marking the birth of the French Revolution and the inauguration of the American federal government.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Aboriginal Australian communities
Interior Southeast Asia beyond Siamese and Vietnamese courts
Pacific Island societies beyond European contact zones
Central Asian steppe polities

events.title: Storming of the Bastille
events.description: On 14 July, a Parisian crowd, joined by mutinous soldiers, stormed the Bastille fortress-prison, killing its governor Bernard-René de Launay. Though the fortress held only seven prisoners, it was a potent symbol of royal absolutism and arbitrary imprisonment. The event marked the point at which the political crisis of the Estates-General became an irreversible popular revolution. It prompted Louis XVI to withdraw royal troops from Paris and became the founding act of revolutionary legitimacy, celebrated to this day as France's national holiday.
key_figures: Bernard-René de Launay (Governor of the Bastille, killed)
Jacques de Flesselles (Prévôt des Marchands, killed)

sources.name: Multiple contemporary eyewitness accounts and pamphlets published in Paris, July 1789
Archives parlementaires de 1787 à 1860
Simon Schama, Citizens: A Chronicle of the French Revolution (1989)

events.certainty_note: One of the most extensively documented single days in European history, with abundant contemporary accounts from multiple perspectives.

events.title: Opening of the Estates-General at Versailles
events.description: On 5 May, Louis XVI convened the Estates-General at Versailles for the first time since 1614, in response to a dire fiscal crisis exacerbated by France's war debts from supporting the American Revolution. The three estates — clergy, nobility, and commons (Third Estate) — immediately clashed over voting procedures. The Third Estate's demand for voting by head rather than by order set the stage for the constitutional confrontation that followed. The convocation was itself a product of years of failed reform attempts by finance ministers Calonne and Loménie de Brienne.
key_figures: Emmanuel-Joseph Sieyès (author of Qu'est-ce que le Tiers-État?)

sources.name: Archives parlementaires de 1787 à 1860
Contemporary published proceedings and pamphlets
Emmanuel-Joseph Sieyès, Qu'est-ce que le Tiers-État? (January 1789)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, private correspondence, and a massive pamphlet literature.

events.title: Third Estate declares itself the National Assembly
events.description: On 17 June, the deputies of the Third Estate, joined by some sympathetic clergy, declared themselves the National Assembly, asserting that they alone represented the French nation. On 20 June, finding their meeting hall locked, they gathered in a nearby indoor tennis court and swore the Tennis Court Oath, pledging not to disband until they had given France a constitution. This act of constitutional self-creation was the legal revolution that preceded the popular revolution of the Bastille. By 27 June, Louis XVI ordered the other two estates to join the National Assembly, tacitly conceding the principle of national sovereignty.
key_figures: Jean-Sylvain Bailly (President of the National Assembly)
Honoré Gabriel Riqueti, comte de Mirabeau (Third Estate leader)

sources.name: Official proceedings of the National Assembly; Jacques-Louis David's painting The Tennis Court Oath (unfinished, 1791)
Timothy Tackett, Becoming a Revolutionary: The Deputies of the French National Assembly (1996)

events.certainty_note: Documented in official assembly records and extensive private correspondence of deputies.

events.title: George Washington inaugurated as first President of the United States
events.description: On 30 April, George Washington was inaugurated as the first President of the United States at Federal Hall in New York City, following his unanimous election by the Electoral College. His inauguration marked the commencement of the federal government under the new Constitution, which had been ratified by the requisite nine states in 1788. Washington's precedent-setting decisions — from the title 'Mr. President' to the formation of a cabinet — shaped the office for centuries. John Adams took office simultaneously as Vice President.
key_figures: Robert R. Livingston (Chancellor of New York, administered the oath)

sources.name: Washington's inaugural address (30 April 1789); contemporary newspaper accounts
Annals of Congress (1st Congress, 1st Session)
Ron Chernow, Washington: A Life (2010)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, private correspondence, and contemporary press.

events.title: Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen adopted
events.description: On 26 August, the National Constituent Assembly adopted the Déclaration des droits de l'homme et du citoyen, a foundational document of modern human rights law. Influenced by Enlightenment philosophy, the American Declaration of Independence, and natural law theory, it proclaimed universal principles of liberty, property, security, resistance to oppression, popular sovereignty, and equality before the law. It became the preamble to the French Constitution of 1791 and profoundly influenced subsequent rights declarations worldwide. Its universalist language, however, did not extend in practice to women, enslaved people, or colonial subjects.
key_figures: Honoré Gabriel Riqueti, comte de Mirabeau (debater)

sources.name: Official text of the Déclaration des droits de l'homme et du citoyen (26 August 1789)
Lynn Hunt, Inventing Human Rights: A History (2007)

events.certainty_note: Original document survives; adoption recorded in Assembly proceedings.

events.title: The Great Fear sweeps rural France
events.description: From approximately 20 July to 6 August, a wave of mass panic known as the Grande Peur swept through the French countryside. Rumors of aristocratic conspiracies, brigand armies, and foreign invasion prompted peasants to arm themselves and attack seigneurial châteaux, burning feudal records and title deeds. The movement reflected deep agrarian grievances over feudal dues, tithes, and land tenure. The Great Fear accelerated the National Assembly's abolition of feudal privileges on the night of 4 August, when nobles dramatically renounced their seigneurial rights in an extraordinary session.
key_figures: Vicomte de Noailles (proposed abolition of feudal rights on 4 August)

sources.name: Georges Lefebvre, La Grande Peur de 1789 (1932)
Contemporary administrative correspondence and municipal records

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through municipal and administrative archives, though the decentralised nature of the panic means no single account captures it all.

events.title: Abolition of feudalism in France (Night of 4 August)
events.description: In a dramatic overnight session on 4–5 August, the National Constituent Assembly voted to abolish the feudal system, including seigneurial rights, tithes, venality of offices, and provincial privileges. The decrees, driven partly by the pressure of the Great Fear, dismantled the legal structure of the ancien régime in a single night. In practice, the abolition was more complex: many feudal dues were declared redeemable rather than simply cancelled, and full implementation took years. Nevertheless, the night of 4 August marked the definitive legal end of feudalism in France.
key_figures: Assembly members of the liberal nobility and clergy

sources.name: Official decrees of the National Assembly, 4–11 August 1789
Michael Fitzsimmons, The Night the Old Regime Ended (2003)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Assembly proceedings. The gap between the sweeping rhetoric of 4 August and the more cautious implementing decrees of 5–11 August is well-studied.

events.title: Women's March on Versailles
events.description: On 5–6 October, thousands of market women, later joined by the National Guard under Lafayette, marched from Paris to Versailles to demand bread and force the royal family to relocate to Paris. After a tense night in which a crowd invaded the palace and killed several royal guards, Louis XVI agreed to move to the Tuileries Palace in Paris.
