The march effectively ended the king's ability to resist the Revolution from the relative safety of Versailles and placed the monarchy under the direct surveillance of the Parisian populace. It also demonstrated the political agency of working-class women in the Revolution.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts (Révolutions de Paris, etc.); police reports
Dominique Godineau, The Women of Paris and Their French Revolution (1998)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in contemporary press, police archives, and subsequent Assembly investigations.

events.title: United States Bill of Rights proposed by Congress
events.description: On 25 September, the First United States Congress approved twelve proposed amendments to the Constitution and sent them to the states for ratification. Drafted primarily by James Madison, the amendments addressed Anti-Federalist concerns about the lack of explicit protections for individual rights. Ten of the twelve were ratified by December 1791, becoming the Bill of Rights. These guaranteed freedoms of speech, religion, press, and assembly; the right to bear arms; protections against unreasonable search and seizure; and due process rights — forming the bedrock of American civil liberties jurisprudence.

sources.name: Annals of Congress (1st Congress, 1st Session); Congressional resolution of 25 September 1789
Richard Labunski, James Madison and the Struggle for the Bill of Rights (2006)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented in Congressional records.

events.title: United States Judiciary Act establishes the federal court system
events.description: Signed into law on 24 September, the Judiciary Act of 1789 established the structure of the federal judiciary, creating a Supreme Court with six justices, thirteen district courts, and three circuit courts. It also established the office of Attorney General and defined the jurisdiction of the federal courts, including the critical Section 25 granting the Supreme Court appellate jurisdiction over state court decisions involving federal law. The Act gave institutional substance to Article III of the Constitution and was largely authored by Senator Oliver Ellsworth of Connecticut.
key_figures: Oliver Ellsworth (principal author, Senator from Connecticut)
John Jay (first Chief Justice, appointed under this framework)

sources.name: Judiciary Act of 1789, 1 Stat. 73
Maeva Marcus, ed., Origins of the Federal Judiciary (1992)

events.certainty_note: Full legislative record survives.

events.title: Mutiny on HMS Bounty
events.description: On 28 April, Fletcher Christian led a mutiny against Lieutenant William Bligh aboard HMS Bounty in the South Pacific, near the Friendly Islands (Tonga). The ship had been on a mission to transport breadfruit plants from Tahiti to the Caribbean as cheap food for enslaved plantation workers. Bligh and eighteen loyalists were set adrift in a small launch; Bligh navigated 3,618 nautical miles to Timor in a remarkable feat of seamanship. The mutineers eventually settled on Pitcairn Island, where their descendants remain. The event became one of the most famous episodes in naval history and raised questions about command culture in the Royal Navy.

sources.name: William Bligh, A Narrative of the Mutiny on Board His Majesty's Ship Bounty (1790)
Admiralty records and courts-martial proceedings
Caroline Alexander, The Bounty: The True Story of the Mutiny on the Bounty (2003)

events.certainty_note: Documented by Bligh's own accounts, crew testimony, and Admiralty records.

events.title: Tipu Sultan's war with Travancore triggers the Third Anglo-Mysore War
events.description: In late December 1789, Tipu Sultan, ruler of the Kingdom of Mysore, attacked the lines of Travancore, a princely state allied with the British East India Company. Tipu's assault on the Travancore Lines (fortified boundary) was prompted by Travancore's purchase of two Dutch forts in Cochin, which Tipu considered within his sphere of influence. Although Tipu's initial assault was repelled, the attack gave the British under Governor-General Lord Cornwallis the casus belli to form a triple alliance with the Marathas and the Nizam of Hyderabad, launching the Third Anglo-Mysore War (1790–1792). The war ended with Tipu's defeat and the Treaty of Seringapatam.

sources.name: East India Company correspondence and dispatches (British Library, India Office Records)
Tipu Sultan's own correspondence (select letters survive in Persian)
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy (1997)

events.certainty_note: Date of the attack on Travancore Lines is well-established in both British and Mysorean sources, though precise dating of early December skirmishes versus the main assault varies slightly.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaign against Gurkha Nepal
events.description: Tensions escalated between the Qing Empire and the newly expansionist Gurkha Kingdom of Nepal over Tibet. Gurkha forces had been encroaching on Tibetan territory and disrupting trade, leading to Tibetan appeals to the Qing court for protection. The Qianlong Emperor, in the final years of his active reign, began organizing a military response that would culminate in the Sino-Nepalese War of 1791–1792, when Qing forces marched through Tibet and to within striking distance of Kathmandu. This conflict reinforced Qing suzerainty over Tibet and resulted in the imposition of the Golden Urn system for selecting reincarnated lamas.
key_figures: Shamarpa Lama (Tibetan religious figure involved in the dispute)

sources.name: Qing court records (Qing Shilu — Veritable Records of the Qing)
Nepalese court chronicles
Michael van Walt van Praag, The Status of Tibet (1987); Peter Schwieger, The Dalai Lama and the Emperor of China (2015)

events.certainty_note: Qing records document the diplomatic crisis well; the precise timeline of early Gurkha incursions in 1788–1789 is less clearly delineated.

events.title: Outbreak of the Brabant Revolution against Habsburg rule
events.description: In October–December 1789, the provinces of the Austrian Netherlands revolted against the centralising reforms of Emperor Joseph II, particularly his abolition of traditional provincial privileges, judicial restructuring, and religious interference. A volunteer army led by Jean-André van der Mersch defeated Austrian forces at the Battle of Turnhout on 27 October. By January 1790, the rebels would declare the United Belgian States. The revolution was internally divided between conservative Statists (led by Hendrik van der Noot) and progressive Vonckists, and was ultimately crushed by Austrian forces under Leopold II in late 1790. It is often overshadowed by the contemporaneous French Revolution but represented a significant anti-Habsburg uprising.

sources.name: Contemporary pamphlets and proclamations of the Brabant revolutionaries
Janet Polasky, Revolution in Brussels 1787–1793 (1987)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in contemporary records, though overshadowed in Anglophone historiography by the French Revolution.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War continues; fall of Belgrade to Austria
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792) and the concurrent Austro-Turkish War (1788–1791) continued. On 8 October 1789, Austrian forces under Field Marshal Laudon captured Belgrade from the Ottomans after a siege, a significant strategic victory. Meanwhile, Russian forces under Suvorov won the Battle of Rymnik (22 September) against the Ottomans in Moldavia, one of his most celebrated victories. These campaigns weakened Ottoman power in southeastern Europe, though Austria would return Belgrade in the Treaty of Sistova (1791) as the distractions of the French Revolution reoriented Habsburg priorities westward.
key_figures: Ernst Gideon von Laudon (Austrian Field Marshal)
Selim III (new Ottoman Sultan from April 1789)

sources.name: Austrian and Russian military dispatches and correspondence
Ottoman chronicle sources, including Ahmed Vasıf Efendi
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870 (2007)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary sources from all belligerents confirm the military events.

events.title: Selim III becomes Ottoman Sultan
events.description: On 7 April, Selim III succeeded his uncle Abdülhamid I as Ottoman Sultan. He inherited an empire at war with both Russia and Austria and in a state of institutional decline. Selim would become known as a reforming sultan, establishing the Nizam-i Cedid (New Order) military forces modelled partly on European armies and attempting administrative modernisation. His reform program ultimately provoked a conservative janissary revolt that deposed him in 1807. His accession marks the beginning of a sustained, if contested, Ottoman reform era.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and contemporary chronicle of Ahmed Vasıf Efendi
Stanford Shaw, Between Old and New: The Ottoman Empire under Sultan Selim III (1971)

events.certainty_note: Sultan's accession date firmly established in Ottoman records.

events.title: First European settlement established in Australia at Sydney Cove
events.description: Though the First Fleet arrived on 26 January 1788, the year 1789 saw the consolidation of the penal colony at Sydney Cove under Governor Arthur Phillip and the onset of devastating consequences for Aboriginal peoples. A catastrophic smallpox epidemic swept through the Aboriginal population of the Sydney basin in April 1789, killing an estimated 50–70% of the local Eora people. The epidemic's origin remains debated — whether introduced by the colonists (possibly from variolous matter carried by surgeons) or through prior Macassan contact in northern Australia. This demographic catastrophe fundamentally shaped the power dynamics of early colonial Australia.
key_figures: Arthur Phillip (Governor of New South Wales)
Bennelong (Eora man, later mediator with the colony)
Arabanoo (Eora man, died in the epidemic)

sources.name: Watkin Tench, A Complete Account of the Settlement at Port Jackson (1793)
David Collins, An Account of the English Colony in New South Wales (1798)
Judy Campbell, Invisible Invaders: Smallpox and Other Diseases in Aboriginal Australia (2002)

events.certainty_note: The epidemic is well-attested in multiple First Fleet journals. Its origin remains debated among historians and epidemiologists.

events.title: Olaudah Equiano publishes his autobiography
events.description: Olaudah Equiano (also known as Gustavus Vassa) published The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano, or Gustavus Vassa, the African, one of the earliest and most influential slave narratives. The book described his childhood in Igboland (modern Nigeria), his kidnapping and enslavement, the horrors of the Middle Passage, and his eventual self-purchase of freedom. It became a bestseller, going through nine editions in his lifetime, and was a powerful weapon in the British abolitionist movement. Equiano actively promoted the book on speaking tours throughout Britain and Ireland.

sources.name: Olaudah Equiano, The Interesting Narrative of the Life of Olaudah Equiano (1789)
Vincent Carretta, Equiano, the African: Biography of a Self-Made Man (2005)

events.certainty_note: The publication is firmly dated. Debate exists over whether Equiano was born in Africa as he claimed or in South Carolina, based on naval and baptismal records discovered by Carretta. This does not affect the publication date or the book's historical significance.

events.title: Antoine Lavoisier publishes Traité Élémentaire de Chimie
events.description: Antoine-Laurent de Lavoisier published his Traité Élémentaire de Chimie (Elementary Treatise of Chemistry), widely considered the first modern chemistry textbook. The work systematised the new chemistry based on the oxygen theory of combustion, definitively replacing the phlogiston theory. It established a rational chemical nomenclature (developed with Guyton de Morveau, Berthollet, and Fourcroy), listed 33 chemical elements, and stated the law of conservation of mass in chemical reactions. The Traité is often cited as marking the birth of modern chemistry as a discipline.
key_figures: Marie-Anne Paulze Lavoisier (illustrator and translator, contributed to the work)

sources.name: Antoine Lavoisier, Traité Élémentaire de Chimie (1789)
Frederic Lawrence Holmes, Antoine Lavoisier: The Next Crucial Year (1998)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and content firmly established. The work itself survives in multiple copies.

events.title: Swedish-Russian War and the mutiny of Anjala
events.description: The Russo-Swedish War (1788–1790) continued with mixed results for Gustaf III of Sweden. In 1789, Gustaf III consolidated power domestically through the Act of Union and Security (passed in February), which strengthened royal authority at the expense of the nobility in the Riksdag. This followed the Anjala conspiracy of 1788, in which Finnish-Swedish officers had mutinied and sought to negotiate separately with Russia. The naval war continued with inconclusive engagements in the Baltic. The domestic political crisis and the external war would both resolve in 1790.

sources.name: Swedish Riksdag proceedings; Gustaf III's correspondence
H. Arnold Barton, Scandinavia in the Revolutionary Era 1760–1815 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Swedish and Russian state archives.

events.title: Tây Sơn dynasty consolidates power in Vietnam
events.description: By 1789, the Tây Sơn dynasty under Nguyễn Huệ (Emperor Quang Trung) had consolidated control over most of Vietnam after a remarkable series of military campaigns. Most notably, in the Lunar New Year offensive of early 1789, Quang Trung decisively defeated a large Chinese Qing expeditionary force that had occupied Thăng Long (Hanoi) in support of the deposed Lê dynasty. The victory at Đống Đa (30 January 1789 by the Gregorian calendar) expelled the Chinese within days of the offensive and is one of the most celebrated military achievements in Vietnamese history. Quang Trung then pursued diplomatic normalisation with the Qing.
key_figures: Nguyễn Huệ / Emperor Quang Trung (Tây Sơn leader)

sources.name: Vietnamese court annals (Đại Việt Sử Ký Toàn Thư tradition; later Tây Sơn-era records)
Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing) — entries on the Vietnam campaign
George Dutton, The Tây Sơn Uprising: Society and Rebellion in Eighteenth-Century Vietnam (2006)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Đống Đa is firmly dated and documented in both Vietnamese and Qing sources, though Vietnamese national historiography has hagiographized Quang Trung considerably.

events.title: French financial crisis and famine exacerbate revolutionary tensions
events.description: The immediate material backdrop to the French Revolution was a severe subsistence crisis. The harvest of 1788 had been catastrophic, partly due to a devastating hailstorm in July 1788 and a severe winter in 1788–1789. By spring 1789, bread prices in Paris reached their highest level in decades, consuming up to 88% of a labourer's wages. The fiscal crisis of the monarchy — a national debt swollen by participation in the American War of Independence and structural inability to tax the privileged orders — had forced the convocation of the Estates-General. The convergence of fiscal collapse, food crisis, and political aspiration was the combustible mixture that made revolution possible.

sources.name: Ernest Labrousse, La crise de l'économie française à la fin de l'Ancien Régime (1944)
Contemporary municipal records on grain prices and police reports on bread riots

events.certainty_note: Grain price data, municipal records, and police reports are abundant and have been extensively analysed by economic historians.

events.title: First U.S. Congress establishes executive departments
events.description: The First Congress of the United States established the key executive departments that gave institutional form to the new federal government. The Department of State (July), Department of War (August), and Department of the Treasury (September) were created by statute, with Washington appointing Thomas Jefferson, Henry Knox, and Alexander Hamilton to head them respectively. The creation of these departments, and the 'Decision of 1789' establishing that the President had sole power to remove executive officers, set crucial precedents for the separation of powers and executive authority.
key_figures: Thomas Jefferson (Secretary of State, took office March 1790)

sources.name: Annals of Congress (1st Congress, 1st Session)
Saikrishna Prakash, Imperial from the Beginning: The Constitution of the Original Executive (2015)

events.certainty_note: Full legislative record survives in the Annals of Congress.

events.title: Endowment of the first Georgetown Academy (later Georgetown University)
events.description: On 23 January 1789, John Carroll, the first Catholic bishop in the United States, established Georgetown Academy, the oldest Jesuit and Catholic institution of higher education in the United States (later Georgetown University). The founding reflected the new religious freedom in the United States under the Constitution and the aspirations of the American Catholic community, which had faced legal disabilities in most colonial jurisdictions. The academy began accepting students in 1792.

sources.name: Georgetown University archives; John Carroll's correspondence
Robert Emmett Curran, A History of Georgetown University (2010)

events.certainty_note: Founding date established in Carroll's correspondence and institutional records.

events.title: William Herschel completes his 40-foot telescope
events.description: William Herschel completed construction of his great 40-foot reflecting telescope at Observatory House in Slough, funded by a grant from King George III. With a 48-inch (122 cm) primary mirror, it was the largest telescope in the world for over fifty years. On its first night of observation (28 August 1789), Herschel discovered Enceladus, a moon of Saturn, and on 17 September he discovered Mimas, another Saturnian moon. Though the telescope proved unwieldy for regular use, it symbolised the era's ambition in astronomical instrumentation.

sources.name: William Herschel's papers and letters to the Royal Society (Philosophical Transactions, 1790)
Michael Hoskin, The Herschel Partnership (2003)

events.certainty_note: Herschel's detailed records of telescope construction and observations survive in the Royal Astronomical Society archives.

disconfirming_evidence: Several points of scholarly dispute apply to events listed: (1) The storming of the Bastille has been mythologized as a liberation of political prisoners, but the fortress held only seven inmates, none of them political prisoners in any meaningful sense — the Bastille's significance was symbolic and strategic (it held gunpowder), not as a mass prison. (2) The Declaration of the Rights of Man was not the purely spontaneous expression of universal ideals it is sometimes portrayed as; Lafayette's draft drew heavily on Jefferson's advice and the Virginia Declaration of Rights, and the final version was the product of contentious committee debate with multiple competing drafts. (3) The 'Night of 4 August' abolition of feudalism was less complete than traditionally presented — the Assembly's implementing decrees of 5–11 August preserved many feudal dues as redeemable (requiring peasant payment), and full abolition of seigneurial rights without compensation did not occur until 1793. (4) Olaudah Equiano's claimed African birth has been challenged by Vincent Carretta, who found a naval muster roll and a baptismal record listing his birthplace as South Carolina; the scholarly community remains divided on whether the African childhood chapters are autobiography or literary construction drawn from other sources. (5) The significance of the Tây Sơn victory at Đống Đa, while militarily real, has been substantially amplified in modern Vietnamese nationalist historiography, and the Qing 'invasion' was more limited in intent and commitment than Vietnamese accounts suggest — the Qianlong Emperor quickly accepted diplomatic face-saving rather than pursuing a prolonged campaign.
historiographic_note: 1789 is one of the most richly documented years in human history, particularly for France and the United States, where abundant contemporary sources survive: legislative proceedings, newspapers, pamphlets, private correspondence, police reports, and diplomatic dispatches. The density of French Revolutionary documentation is extraordinary. However, this very richness creates interpretive challenges: the Revolution generated massive propaganda from all factions, and later political uses of 1789 (by republicans, socialists, liberals, and conservatives alike) have layered interpretation over evidence. Coverage is heavily Euro-Atlantic. Asian events (Qing, Ottoman, Vietnamese, Indian) are documented primarily through court chronicles and colonial records, which carry their own biases. Sub-Saharan African, Pacific Island, and Central Asian histories for this year are largely invisible in the documentary record, existing primarily through later oral traditions, archaeological inference, or European contact accounts that filter indigenous events through colonial perspectives.

graph_edges.note: The storming of the Bastille began the revolutionary process that culminated in the king's trial and execution.
The Declaration of the Rights of Man is a direct ancestor document of the UDHR.
Washington's inauguration followed from the ratification of the Constitution.
Congressional proposal of amendments led to state ratification as the Bill of Rights.
Tipu's attack on Travancore triggered the Third Anglo-Mysore War, concluded by this treaty.
The fiscal and subsistence crisis was the material precondition for the revolutionary explosion.
The Estates-General's procedural deadlock led directly to the Third Estate's self-declaration as National Assembly.
The Great Fear's rural violence pressured the Assembly into the abolition of feudal privileges.
Selim III's reform agenda, begun at accession, provoked the conservative reaction that deposed him.
Tây Sơn power peaked in 1789 but was eventually overthrown by the Nguyễn restoration.
Lavoisier's scientific triumph in 1789 was followed by his execution during the Terror due to his role as a tax farmer.
The 1789 smallpox epidemic followed from the establishment of the British colony in 1788.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The late 18th century saw the converging crises of the Atlantic Revolutions. In North America, the newly independent United States was transitioning from the Articles of Confederation to the Constitution. France's Ancien Régime was collapsing under fiscal insolvency, leading toward the Revolution of 1789. Britain was expanding its global empire, establishing a penal colony in Australia. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained the world's most populous state, while the Ottoman Empire fought a two-front war against Russia and Austria.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond Sierra Leone
Southeast Asia (Siam, Burma, Vietnam)
Central Asia and Inner Asian khanates
Pacific Islands beyond European contact zones
Indigenous Australian perspectives on colonization

events.title: First Fleet arrives at Botany Bay and Sydney Cove
events.description: The British First Fleet of eleven ships, commanded by Captain Arthur Phillip, arrived at Botany Bay on 18–20 January 1788, carrying approximately 750 convicts and 550 crew and marines. Finding Botany Bay unsuitable, Phillip relocated to Port Jackson (Sydney Cove), where the colony was formally established on 26 January. This marked the beginning of British colonization of Australia and the catastrophic dispossession of Aboriginal peoples who had inhabited the continent for over 65,000 years. The colony endured severe food shortages in its first years.
key_figures: Arthur Phillip (Captain-General and Governor-designate of New South Wales)
Lord Sydney (Secretary of State for Home Affairs)

sources.name: Arthur Phillip, The Voyage of Governor Phillip to Botany Bay (1789)
Watkin Tench, A Narrative of the Expedition to Botany Bay (1789)
John White, Journal of a Voyage to New South Wales (1790)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary first-hand accounts survive from officers, marines, and officials in the First Fleet.

events.title: US Constitution ratified by requisite nine states
events.description: Over the course of 1788, the ratification debates for the US Constitution reached their climax. New Hampshire became the ninth state to ratify on 21 June 1788, meeting the threshold for the Constitution to take effect. Virginia ratified four days later on 25 June, and New York followed on 26 July after fierce debate. The ratification process produced the Federalist Papers (Hamilton, Madison, Jay) and Anti-Federalist writings that remain foundational texts of American political thought. The Confederation Congress set dates for the new government to commence in March 1789.
key_figures: George Clinton (Governor of New York, Anti-Federalist)

sources.name: The Federalist Papers, published 1787–1788 in New York newspapers
Jonathan Elliot, The Debates in the Several State Conventions on the Adoption of the Federal Constitution (1836)
Journals of the Continental Congress

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary newspapers, official records, and personal correspondence.

events.title: Day of Tiles in Grenoble
events.description: On 7 June 1788, residents of Grenoble rioted against royal troops sent to enforce the exile of the Parlement of Dauphiné, hurling roof tiles at soldiers from the city's rooftops. This uprising, the 'Journée des Tuiles,' was one of the first acts of popular resistance against royal authority in the pre-revolutionary crisis. It led directly to the Assembly of Vizille on 21 July, where the three estates of Dauphiné met together and demanded the convocation of the Estates-General, setting a precedent for the revolutionary movement.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper accounts and pamphlets from Dauphiné
Jean Egret, The French Pre-Revolution 1787–1788 (historiographic study)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in contemporary sources and considered a key event in pre-revolutionary historiography.

events.title: Lamoignon Edicts and the Parlementary Crisis
events.description: In May 1788, Keeper of the Seals Chrétien-François de Lamoignon attempted to break the resistance of the parlements by issuing edicts that stripped them of their power to register royal legislation, transferring this authority to a new Plenary Court. The parlements and provincial estates erupted in protest across France. The edicts provoked widespread unrest and deepened the fiscal and political crisis, contributing to the decision by Louis XVI and his chief minister Loménie de Brienne to convoke the Estates-General. In August, Brienne resigned and was replaced by Jacques Necker; the Lamoignon edicts were withdrawn in September.
key_figures: Chrétien-François de Lamoignon (Keeper of the Seals)
Étienne-Charles de Loménie de Brienne (Principal Minister)

sources.name: Registres du Parlement de Paris
William Doyle, The Oxford History of the French Revolution (1989)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, parliamentary registers, and contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War continues; siege of Ochakov
events.region: Ottoman Empire / Russian Empire (Black Sea region)
events.description: The Russo-Turkish War (1787–1792) continued with the Russian siege of the Ottoman fortress of Ochakov (Özi) on the Black Sea coast. After a prolonged siege through harsh winter conditions, Russian forces under Grigory Potemkin stormed the fortress on 17 December 1788 in a bloody assault. The capture of Ochakov was a major Russian strategic victory, securing control of the northern Black Sea coast and the Bug-Dniester littoral. Ottoman losses were heavy, with thousands killed during the assault.

sources.name: Russian military dispatches and Potemkin's correspondence with Catherine II
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870: An Empire Besieged (2007)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in both Russian and Ottoman sources. Date of storming firmly established.

events.title: Austria declares war on the Ottoman Empire
events.description: In February 1788, Emperor Joseph II of Austria entered the war against the Ottoman Empire as an ally of Russia, in accordance with their 1781 defensive alliance. The Austrian campaign was poorly managed, with an ill-prepared army suffering from logistical failures and disease. The disastrous 'Battle of Karánsebes' (possibly September 1788) became legendary as an incident of friendly fire, though its historical details are disputed. Austrian forces failed to achieve significant objectives in 1788, and Joseph II himself fell ill during the campaign.
key_figures: Field Marshal Franz Moritz von Lacy (Austrian commander)

sources.name: Austrian Kriegsarchiv military records
Derek Beales, Joseph II: Against the World, 1780–1790 (2009)

events.certainty_note: Austrian entry into the war is well-documented. Specific battle details (especially Karánsebes) are less certain — see disconfirming evidence.

events.title: Russo-Swedish War begins
events.description: King Gustav III of Sweden launched a war against Russia in June 1788, seeking to exploit Russia's engagement with the Ottoman Empire to recover territories lost since the Great Northern War. The Swedish navy fought the Battle of Hogland (17 July) against the Russian Baltic Fleet, an indecisive engagement. The war was complicated by the Anjala Conspiracy, in which a group of Swedish-Finnish officers mutinied and sought to negotiate separately with Russia, nearly toppling Gustav III's authority. The war would continue until 1790.

sources.name: Swedish Riksarkivet records and Gustav III's correspondence
R.C. Anderson, Naval Wars in the Baltic (1910, revised 1969)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Swedish, Russian, and Finnish archives.
cross_references: 1787-related: Russo-Turkish War weakens Russian position in Baltic

events.title: Anjala Conspiracy among Swedish-Finnish officers
events.description: In August 1788, over a hundred Swedish-Finnish officers at the Anjala camp signed a petition (the Anjala Note or Liikkala Note) refusing to continue Gustav III's offensive war against Russia and seeking direct negotiations with Catherine II. The conspirators argued the war was unconstitutional since it was offensive and had not been approved by the Riksdag. The conspiracy ultimately failed — Gustav III rallied domestic support and convened the Riksdag in early 1789, where he strengthened royal power. Several conspirators were later arrested and one, Colonel Hästesko, was executed.

sources.name: The Anjala/Liikkala Note (original document)
Finnish and Swedish historiographic tradition

events.certainty_note: The original documents survive and the event is well-attested in multiple archives.

events.title: Impeachment trial of Warren Hastings begins
events.description: The impeachment trial of Warren Hastings, former Governor-General of Bengal, opened before the House of Lords on 13 February 1788. Edmund Burke led the prosecution, delivering a four-day opening speech accusing Hastings of corruption, extortion, and misgovernment in India. The trial, which would not conclude until 1795 (ending in acquittal), became a landmark event in British parliamentary history and in the debate over imperial governance and accountability. It raised fundamental questions about the responsibilities of colonial administrators.

sources.name: Parliamentary records and trial transcripts in the House of Lords
P.J. Marshall, The Impeachment of Warren Hastings (1965)

events.certainty_note: Extensively recorded in parliamentary proceedings and contemporary press.

events.title: Province of Freedom settlement collapses in Sierra Leone
events.description: The 'Province of Freedom,' established in 1787 by the Committee for the Relief of the Black Poor with approximately 400 settlers (mostly Black Loyalists and Black Poor from London), largely collapsed by 1788. Disease, hostile relations with the local Temne under King Tom and his successor King Jimmy, and the destruction of the settlement by King Jimmy's forces in December 1789 devastated the colony. By late 1788, the settler population had been reduced dramatically. This failure would lead to the reconstitution of the colony under the Sierra Leone Company in 1792.

sources.name: Letters and reports from Granville Sharp and settlers
Christopher Fyfe, A History of Sierra Leone (1962)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary letters and reports document the settlement's difficulties, though exact casualty figures are uncertain.

events.title: French convocation of the Estates-General announced
events.description: On 8 August 1788, Louis XVI announced the convocation of the Estates-General for 1 May 1789, the first meeting of this body since 1614. The decision was forced by the complete collapse of royal finances — the Treasury was empty and the government could not borrow further. Necker was recalled as finance minister on 25 August. The announcement triggered an explosion of political pamphleteering and debate over the form of representation, particularly whether the Third Estate should have double representation and whether voting should be by order or by head.

sources.name: Royal decree of 8 August 1788
Georges Lefebvre, The Coming of the French Revolution (1947)

events.certainty_note: The royal decree and surrounding political events are thoroughly documented.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaigns in Taiwan
events.description: The Lin Shuangwen rebellion in Taiwan (1787–1788), one of the largest uprisings in Qing-era Taiwan, was suppressed by Qing imperial forces under Fukang'an in early 1788. Lin Shuangwen, leader of a Heaven and Earth Society (Tiandihui) uprising, was captured in February 1788 and later executed in Beijing. The Qianlong Emperor counted the suppression among his 'Ten Great Campaigns.' The rebellion exposed weaknesses in Qing frontier administration and the growing tensions between immigrant Han Chinese settlers and both indigenous Taiwanese and Qing authorities.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
John Robert Shepherd, Statecraft and Political Economy on the Taiwan Frontier 1600–1800 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Qing imperial records, local gazetteers, and official campaign histories.

events.title: HMS Bounty departs England for Tahiti
events.description: HMS Bounty, under the command of Lieutenant William Bligh, departed from Spithead on 23 December 1787 (arriving at Tahiti on 26 October 1788) on a mission to collect breadfruit plants for transplantation to the West Indies as a cheap food source for enslaved laborers. The voyage would culminate in the famous mutiny of April 1789 led by Fletcher Christian. The Bounty's mission exemplified the intersection of imperial botany, the slave economy, and Pacific exploration that characterized late 18th-century British maritime activity.
key_figures: Sir Joseph Banks (patron and planner of the expedition)

sources.name: William Bligh, A Voyage to the South Sea (1792)
Admiralty records, The National Archives (Kew)

events.certainty_note: Bligh's detailed log and official Admiralty records provide precise dates.

events.title: Great Hail Storm devastates France
events.description: On 13 July 1788, a catastrophic hailstorm struck a broad swath of France from Normandy to the south, destroying crops across large areas at a critical point in the growing season. Combined with the preceding drought, this event caused a severe grain shortage and soaring bread prices in the winter of 1788–89. The resulting subsistence crisis was a direct contributing factor to the popular unrest and anger that fed into the French Revolution. The harvest failure made the political crisis inseparable from an economic one.

sources.name: Contemporary meteorological observations and intendant reports
Emmanuel Le Roy Ladurie, Histoire humaine et comparée du climat (2004–2009)

events.certainty_note: Documented in numerous contemporary administrative reports and later confirmed by climate historians.

events.title: South Carolina, Maryland, and Georgia ratify the US Constitution
events.description: In early 1788, several key states ratified the US Constitution: Georgia (2 January, unanimously), Connecticut (9 January), Massachusetts (6 February, narrowly), Maryland (28 April), and South Carolina (23 May). Massachusetts's close vote (187–168) was secured only by the promise of subsequent amendments (the future Bill of Rights), a compromise that proved crucial to winning over other skeptical states. These ratifications built the momentum that carried the Constitution past the nine-state threshold in June.
key_figures: John Hancock (Governor of Massachusetts, whose support was pivotal)
Samuel Adams (Massachusetts delegate, persuaded to support ratification)

sources.name: State convention records and Elliot's Debates
Pauline Maier, Ratification: The People Debate the Constitution, 1787–1788 (2010)

events.certainty_note: Official state records provide exact dates and vote tallies.

events.title: Bread riots and unrest across France
events.description: Throughout late 1788 and into 1789, France experienced widespread bread riots and popular unrest driven by soaring grain prices following the harvest failure. The price of bread, the dietary staple of the common people, reached levels that consumed the majority of a laborer's wages. Riots broke out in numerous cities and towns. The subsistence crisis intersected with the political crisis over the Estates-General, radicalizing the urban poor and peasantry and creating the volatile social conditions that would explode in 1789.

sources.name: Intendant reports and police records from various French provinces
George Rudé, The Crowd in the French Revolution (1959)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in administrative records, though individual riot details vary in completeness.

events.title: Abolition movement gains momentum in Britain
events.description: In 1788, the British abolition movement intensified. The Privy Council began hearings on the slave trade in February, collecting extensive testimony. William Dolben's Act (the Slave Trade Act 1788) was passed in July, the first parliamentary legislation to regulate conditions on slave ships by limiting the number of enslaved persons per ton of ship capacity. Though a modest measure, it represented the first legislative crack in the edifice of the slave trade and demonstrated the growing political power of the abolitionist campaign led by William Wilberforce and the Society for Effecting the Abolition of the Slave Trade.
key_figures: William Dolben (MP, sponsor of the bill)

sources.name: Parliamentary debates and Dolben's Act text (28 Geo. III c.54)
Adam Hochschild, Bury the Chains (2005)

events.certainty_note: Parliamentary records and extensive abolitionist correspondence provide thorough documentation.

events.title: Tripolitanian-Tunisian War
events.description: Ali Pasha Karamanli of Tripoli launched an invasion of Tunisia in 1788, seeking to exploit the weakness of the Husainid bey of Tunis. The conflict drew in various regional actors and reflected the complex semi-autonomous politics of the Ottoman Maghreb, where local dynasties pursued their own territorial ambitions while maintaining nominal Ottoman suzerainty. The war disrupted Mediterranean trade and drew European diplomatic attention, though it remained largely a regional affair.

sources.name: European consular dispatches from Tripoli and Tunis
Jamil Abun-Nasr, A History of the Maghrib in the Islamic Period (1987)

events.certainty_note: Documented in European consular records and later Arabic histories, though exact chronology of campaigns is uncertain.

events.title: Tipu Sultan negotiates with France
events.description: Tipu Sultan of Mysore, the most powerful Indian ruler resisting British expansion, sent an embassy to the court of Louis XVI in 1787–1788, seeking a French alliance against the British East India Company. The ambassadors arrived in France and were received, but the French, consumed by their own fiscal crisis, could offer little concrete military support. The failure to secure a meaningful French alliance left Tipu strategically isolated and would contribute to his difficulties in the Third Anglo-Mysore War (1789–1792).

sources.name: French diplomatic archives and Mysore court records
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy (1997)

events.certainty_note: Both French and Mysorean sources document the embassy, though details of negotiations are incomplete.

events.title: Charles IV becomes King of Spain
events.description: Charles III of Spain died on 14 December 1788, and was succeeded by his son Charles IV. The new king inherited the world's largest colonial empire but lacked his father's political abilities. Charles IV's reliance on his wife María Luisa and the favorite Manuel Godoy would lead to factional instability at court and Spain's entanglement in the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, ultimately contributing to the collapse of Spanish authority in the Americas.

sources.name: Spanish court records and diplomatic correspondence
John Lynch, Bourbon Spain 1700–1808 (1989)

events.certainty_note: Date of death and succession are firmly established in official records.

events.title: New York City serves as temporary US capital
events.description: The Confederation Congress, sitting in New York City, passed legislation in September 1788 setting the dates for the first presidential election and the commencement of the new federal government under the Constitution. The first Wednesday of January 1789 was set for choosing presidential electors, the first Wednesday of February for the electoral vote, and the first Wednesday of March for the new Congress to convene. New York City was designated as the temporary seat of the federal government.
key_figures: Cyrus Griffin (President of the Continental Congress)

sources.name: Journals of the Continental Congress, September 1788

events.certainty_note: Official congressional journals provide exact dates and resolutions.

events.title: La Pérouse expedition last seen at Botany Bay
events.description: The French exploration expedition of Jean-François de La Pérouse, which had been circumnavigating the Pacific since 1785, arrived at Botany Bay on 26 January 1788, just days after the British First Fleet. La Pérouse remained for six weeks, departing in March 1788. This was the last confirmed sighting of the expedition; both ships (Boussole and Astrolabe) were subsequently lost, likely wrecked on reefs at Vanikoro in the Solomon Islands. The mystery of their fate was not resolved until wreckage was found in 1826–1827.
key_figures: Jean-François de Galaup, comte de La Pérouse (expedition commander)

sources.name: Arthur Phillip's despatches noting La Pérouse's arrival
John Dunmore, Where Fate Beckons: The Life of Jean-François de La Pérouse (2006)

events.certainty_note: La Pérouse's presence at Botany Bay is confirmed by multiple British witnesses. The circumstances of the expedition's loss remain partially reconstructed.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Karánsebes (1788-006), often described as one of history's most absurd friendly fire incidents — where Austrian troops supposedly routed themselves — is likely apocryphal or greatly exaggerated. The earliest detailed account appears in a much later Austrian history (1831), and no contemporary source describes the battle in the farcical terms commonly repeated. Modern military historians treat the popular version with considerable skepticism. Regarding the First Fleet (1788-001), the traditional narrative of a founding moment is contested by Indigenous Australian perspectives and scholarship that frames 26 January as the beginning of invasion, dispossession, and genocide rather than 'settlement.' The celebratory framing of Australia Day on this date remains deeply controversial. The Hastings impeachment (1788-009) is sometimes portrayed as a triumph of parliamentary accountability, but revisionist scholarship notes that the charges were politically motivated by the Fox-North coalition's rivalry with Pitt, and that many of the specific allegations were exaggerated or taken out of context. Hastings was ultimately acquitted on all charges.
historiographic_note: 1788 is exceptionally well-documented in Western sources — parliamentary records, diplomatic correspondence, military dispatches, and private letters survive in abundance for European, American, and colonial events. However, this richness creates a strong Eurocentric bias in the available record. The perspectives of Aboriginal Australians encountering colonization, enslaved Africans in the Atlantic system, and common people in Asian empires are largely absent from the archives. Ottoman and Qing records exist but are less thoroughly integrated into English-language historiography. The French pre-revolutionary period is particularly well-studied but interpretively contested — Marxist, revisionist, and post-revisionist schools read the same events very differently.

graph_edges.note: First Fleet departed England May 1787, arrived January 1788
Constitution drafted at Philadelphia Convention 1787, ratified through 1788
Day of Tiles and Assembly of Vizille were precursors to the Revolution
Failure of Lamoignon edicts forced convocation of Estates-General
Siege of Ochakov was a major operation in the ongoing Russo-Turkish War
Gustav III's war against Russia provoked the Anjala Conspiracy
Announcement of Estates-General set stage for revolutionary politics
Bounty's 1788 voyage to Tahiti preceded the April 1789 mutiny
Hailstorm destroyed crops, directly causing the bread crisis
Failed French alliance left Tipu Sultan isolated against British
Charles IV's weak reign culminated in Napoleonic invasion of Spain
La Pérouse arrived at Botany Bay the same day as the First Fleet

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The late 1780s were a period of profound political experimentation and crisis across the Atlantic world. The United States, independent for barely a decade, grappled with the inadequacy of the Articles of Confederation. France teetered on the edge of fiscal collapse under Louis XVI, with the Assembly of Notables failing to resolve the crisis that would erupt into revolution within two years. The Ottoman Empire fought Russia and Austria, the Qing dynasty under Qianlong entered its twilight of prosperity, and European colonial powers continued to expand commercial and territorial reach in Asia, Africa, and the Pacific.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading enclaves
Interior Australia and Oceania beyond European contact zones
Central Asian steppe polities
Most of Southeast Asia outside European-controlled ports

events.title: United States Constitutional Convention opens in Philadelphia
events.description: On 25 May 1787, delegates from twelve of the thirteen states (Rhode Island refused to attend) convened in Philadelphia to revise the Articles of Confederation. Under the presidency of George Washington and the intellectual leadership of James Madison, the convention instead drafted an entirely new Constitution establishing a federal republic with separated powers, a bicameral legislature, an independent executive, and a federal judiciary. The document was signed on 17 September 1787 and sent to the states for ratification. The convention's work created the oldest written national constitution still in force and profoundly influenced constitutional design worldwide.
key_figures: Gouverneur Morris (Pennsylvania delegate, penman of final draft)
Edmund Randolph (Virginia governor, presented Virginia Plan)
Roger Sherman (Connecticut delegate, Great Compromise architect)

sources.name: James Madison, Notes of Debates in the Federal Convention of 1787 (published 1840, written contemporaneously)
Official journal of the Convention (published 1819)
Robert Yates, Secret Proceedings and Debates of the Convention (published 1821)
Max Farrand, The Records of the Federal Convention of 1787 (1911)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by multiple participant accounts. Madison's notes, though published posthumously, were taken during the sessions and are considered the most complete record.

events.title: Northwest Ordinance enacted by the Confederation Congress
events.description: On 13 July 1787, the Congress of the Confederation passed the Northwest Ordinance, establishing governance for the territory north of the Ohio River and east of the Mississippi. The ordinance created a process by which territories could achieve statehood on equal footing with existing states, prohibited slavery in the territory, guaranteed civil liberties including trial by jury and religious freedom, and mandated support for public education. It became a template for American westward expansion and is considered one of the most consequential legislative acts of the Confederation era, even though it was enacted by the same Congress whose inadequacy the Constitutional Convention was simultaneously attempting to remedy.
key_figures: Manasseh Cutler (Ohio Company lobbyist who influenced provisions)

sources.name: Journals of the Continental Congress, 1774–1789, Vol. 32–33
Peter Onuf, Statehood and Union: A History of the Northwest Ordinance (1987)

events.certainty_note: Congressional records and contemporary correspondence provide thorough documentation.

events.title: Federalist Papers publication begins
events.description: Beginning on 27 October 1787, Alexander Hamilton, James Madison, and John Jay published the first of 85 essays collectively known as The Federalist Papers in New York newspapers under the pseudonym 'Publius.' Written to persuade New York voters to support ratification of the new Constitution, the essays provided a systematic defense of federalism, separation of powers, and republican government. They remain among the most important texts in American political philosophy and are frequently cited in constitutional interpretation by the US Supreme Court.

sources.name: Original newspaper publications in The Independent Journal, New York Packet, and The Daily Advertiser (1787–1788)
Jacob Cooke, ed., The Federalist (1961, critical edition)

events.certainty_note: Original newspaper publications survive. Authorship of individual papers was disputed until the 20th century; modern statistical and historical analysis has largely resolved attribution.

events.title: Assembly of Notables convenes in France
events.description: On 22 February 1787, Louis XVI convened an Assembly of Notables — 144 members of the aristocracy, clergy, and magistracy — at Versailles to approve fiscal reforms proposed by Controller-General Charles Alexandre de Calonne. Calonne proposed a universal land tax, abolition of internal customs barriers, and provincial assemblies. The Notables refused to approve the taxes, challenged Calonne's financial figures, and demanded to see the royal accounts. Calonne was dismissed in April and replaced by Loménie de Brienne, who fared no better. The Assembly was dissolved on 25 May without resolving the fiscal crisis, pushing France toward the convocation of the Estates-General and ultimately the Revolution of 1789.
key_figures: Charles Alexandre de Calonne (Controller-General of Finances)
Étienne Charles de Loménie de Brienne (Archbishop of Toulouse, successor to Calonne)

sources.name: Procès-verbaux de l'Assemblée des Notables (1787)
William Doyle, The Oxford History of the French Revolution (2002)
Vivian Gruder, The Notables and the Nation (2007)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in official proceedings, contemporary correspondence, and memoirs.

events.title: Parlement of Paris exiled after refusing royal edicts
events.description: After the failure of the Assembly of Notables, Loménie de Brienne attempted to push fiscal reform through the Parlement of Paris, the kingdom's most powerful law court. The Parlement refused to register new tax edicts and called for the convening of the Estates-General — a body that had not met since 1614. In August 1787, Louis XVI exiled the Parlement to Troyes as punishment. Public opinion rallied to the parlementaires, and the king was forced to recall them in September after a face-saving compromise. The confrontation demonstrated the monarchy's inability to overcome institutional resistance to reform and further delegitimized royal authority.
key_figures: Members of the Parlement of Paris

sources.name: Remonstrances du Parlement de Paris au XVIIIe siècle (edited by Flammermont, 1888–1898)
Jean Egret, The French Pre-Revolution 1787–1788 (English trans. 1977)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in parliamentary records, royal decrees, and contemporary press.

events.title: Russo-Turkish War of 1787–1792 begins
events.region: Ottoman Empire / Russian Empire (Black Sea region)
events.description: In August 1787, the Ottoman Empire declared war on Russia, seeking to reverse the losses of the Treaty of Küçük Kaynarca (1774) and the Russian annexation of Crimea (1783). The immediate trigger was Russian interference in Ottoman affairs and Catherine the Great's provocative tour of Crimea earlier in the year. The war would last until 1792 and result in further Ottoman territorial losses, consolidated Russian control of the northern Black Sea coast, and the Treaty of Jassy. Austria joined as Russia's ally in 1788.

sources.name: Ottoman court chronicles (Vak'anüvis records)
Russian diplomatic and military correspondence
Virginia Aksan, Ottoman Wars 1700–1870 (2007)

events.certainty_note: War declaration and major campaigns documented in Ottoman, Russian, and European diplomatic archives.

events.title: Catherine the Great's tour of Crimea and southern Russia
events.description: In January–July 1787, Catherine II undertook a lavish six-month tour of Russia's recently acquired southern territories, including Crimea. Organized by Grigory Potemkin, the tour was designed to demonstrate Russian mastery over the region to foreign diplomats, including Emperor Joseph II of Austria who joined the journey. The tour became the origin of the phrase 'Potemkin village' — the claim that Potemkin constructed fake façades to impress the Empress. The spectacle alarmed the Ottoman Empire and contributed to the outbreak of war later that year.
key_figures: Joseph II (Holy Roman Emperor, travel companion)

sources.name: Catherine II's correspondence and travel journals
Comte de Ségur, Mémoires, ou Souvenirs et Anecdotes (1824–1826)
Simon Sebag Montefiore, Prince of Princes: The Life of Potemkin (2000)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by participants and foreign diplomats. The 'Potemkin village' story is attested but its truth is debated — see disconfirming evidence section.

events.title: Dutch Patriot crisis and Prussian invasion of the Netherlands
events.description: The Patriot movement, which sought to reduce the power of Stadtholder William V of Orange and democratize the Dutch Republic, had effectively controlled much of the country since 1785. In June 1787, Patriot militia detained Princess Wilhelmina of Prussia (William V's wife and sister of Frederick William II of Prussia) near Gouda. This insult to Prussian royal honor provoked Frederick William II to invade the Dutch Republic in September with 20,000 troops under the Duke of Brunswick. The Prussian army quickly crushed Patriot resistance, restored William V to power, and sent thousands of Patriots into exile — many to France, where they would later support the French Revolution.
key_figures: William V (Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic)
Charles William Ferdinand, Duke of Brunswick (Prussian commander)

sources.name: Contemporary Dutch and Prussian diplomatic correspondence
Simon Schama, Patriots and Liberators: Revolution in the Netherlands 1780–1813 (1977)
Joost Rosendaal, Bataven! Nederlandse vluchtelingen in Frankrijk 1787–1795 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in diplomatic archives and contemporary press across Europe.

events.title: First Fleet departs England for Botany Bay
events.description: On 13 May 1787, the First Fleet of eleven ships under Captain Arthur Phillip departed Portsmouth, carrying approximately 750 convicts and 550 crew and marines to establish a penal colony in New South Wales. The fleet would arrive at Botany Bay on 18–20 January 1788 and ultimately settle at Sydney Cove on 26 January 1788. This voyage marked the beginning of British colonization of Australia, with catastrophic long-term consequences for Aboriginal peoples who had inhabited the continent for at least 65,000 years.
key_figures: Arthur Phillip (Captain, First Fleet commander, later first Governor of New South Wales)

sources.name: Arthur Phillip, The Voyage of Governor Phillip to Botany Bay (1789)
John White, Journal of a Voyage to New South Wales (1790)
Robert Hughes, The Fatal Shore (1987)

events.certainty_note: Multiple ship logs, journals, and official records document the departure and voyage in detail.

events.title: Shays' Rebellion effectively ends
events.description: The armed uprising of indebted farmers in western Massachusetts, led by Daniel Shays, which had erupted in late 1786, was effectively suppressed in early 1787. On 25 January, Shays led an attack on the federal arsenal at Springfield, which was repelled by militia under General William Shepard. Shays' forces were routed at Petersham on 4 February by an army funded by Boston merchants and led by General Benjamin Lincoln. The rebellion alarmed the political elite nationwide and became a powerful argument for a stronger central government, directly influencing the decision to call the Constitutional Convention.

sources.name: Contemporary Massachusetts government records and correspondence
Leonard Richards, Shays's Rebellion: The American Revolution's Final Battle (2002)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in government records, military dispatches, and personal correspondence of the period.

events.title: Sierra Leone founded as settlement for freed slaves
events.description: In April 1787, approximately 400 'Black Poor' — freed African Americans, Black Loyalists from the American Revolution, and some white women — sailed from England to establish a settlement called the 'Province of Freedom' on the Sierra Leone peninsula, on land acquired from the local Temne chief King Tom. The venture was organized by the Committee for the Relief of the Black Poor with the support of abolitionist Granville Sharp. The settlement struggled with disease, hostile relations with local rulers, and poor planning; it was largely destroyed by 1789 but was re-established as Freetown by the Sierra Leone Company in 1792.
key_figures: Henry Smeathman (original promoter, died before voyage)

sources.name: Granville Sharp's correspondence and journal
Anna Maria Falconbridge, Narrative of Two Voyages to the River Sierra Leone (1794)
Cassandra Pybus, Epic Journeys of Freedom (2006)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British government records, abolitionist society minutes, and participant accounts.

events.title: Matsudaira Sadanobu begins Kansei Reforms in Japan
events.description: In mid-1787, Matsudaira Sadanobu became senior councillor (rōjū shuseki) of the Tokugawa shogunate following rice riots in Edo and other cities and the disgrace of the previous chief minister Tanuma Okitsugu. Sadanobu launched the Kansei Reforms, a program of fiscal austerity, moral rearmament, cancellation of samurai debts to merchants, restrictions on heterodox scholarship, and sumptuary laws. The reforms represented a conservative reaction against the perceived corruption and cultural permissiveness of the Tanuma era and would shape shogunal policy until Sadanobu's own fall from power in 1793.

sources.name: Matsudaira Sadanobu, Uge no Hitokoto (autobiographical reflections)
Tokugawa jikki (official chronicle of the Tokugawa shoguns)
Herman Ooms, Charismatic Bureaucrat: A Political Biography of Matsudaira Sadanobu (1975)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in shogunal records, Sadanobu's own writings, and contemporary accounts. Exact start date of reforms is debated within a few months.

events.title: Tipu Sultan consolidates power in Mysore
events.description: By 1787, Tipu Sultan had consolidated his position as ruler of Mysore following his father Hyder Ali's death in 1782. He implemented administrative, military, and economic reforms including a new calendar, revenue system, and trade policies. He sought alliances with France and the Ottoman Empire to counter British expansion. Tipu also launched military campaigns against neighboring states and chieftains, including the Kodagu (Coorg) rajas. His military innovations, including the extensive use of iron-cased rockets, influenced later developments in rocketry. He was simultaneously expanding Mysorean influence and provoking the coalition of enemies — the British, Nizam, and Marathas — that would eventually destroy his kingdom.

sources.name: Tipu Sultan's correspondence (preserved in British archives and Mysore palace records)
Kate Brittlebank, Tipu Sultan's Search for Legitimacy (1997)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Mysorean administrative records and extensive British East India Company correspondence.
cross_references: 1799-Fourth Anglo-Mysore War and death of Tipu Sultan

events.title: Three-Fifths Compromise adopted at Constitutional Convention
events.description: On 12 July 1787, delegates to the Constitutional Convention reached the Three-Fifths Compromise, stipulating that enslaved persons would be counted as three-fifths of a free person for purposes of congressional representation and direct taxation. The compromise resolved an impasse between Northern and Southern states but embedded the institution of slavery into the constitutional framework, granting slaveholding states disproportionate political power in the House of Representatives and the Electoral College. It remained in effect until the Fourteenth Amendment (1868).

sources.name: James Madison, Notes of Debates in the Federal Convention of 1787
Garry Wills, 'Negro President': Jefferson and the Slave Power (2003)

events.certainty_note: Recorded in convention proceedings. The ratio itself originated earlier in a 1783 congressional proposal for taxation.

events.title: MV Lavoisier publishes decomposition of water
events.description: In 1787, Antoine Lavoisier, along with collaborators including Claude Louis Berthollet, Antoine François de Fourcroy, and Louis-Bernard Guyton de Morveau, published the Méthode de nomenclature chimique, which established the modern system of chemical nomenclature. The work formalized naming conventions based on elemental composition rather than alchemical tradition, introducing systematic names such as 'oxygen' and 'hydrogen.' This publication was a cornerstone of the Chemical Revolution, replacing phlogiston theory with Lavoisier's oxygen-based framework for understanding combustion and composition.

sources.name: Guyton de Morveau, Lavoisier, Berthollet, and Fourcroy, Méthode de nomenclature chimique (1787)
Bernadette Bensaude-Vincent, Lavoisier: Mémoires d'une révolution (1993)

events.certainty_note: The publication survives and its date is uncontested.

events.title: Mozart completes Eine kleine Nachtmusik
events.description: On 10 August 1787, Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart completed his Serenade No. 13 in G major, K. 525, known as Eine kleine Nachtmusik (A Little Night Music). Mozart also composed the opera Don Giovanni during this year, premiering it in Prague on 29 October to great acclaim. The year 1787 was one of Mozart's most productive, but also personally difficult — his father Leopold died on 28 May, and he struggled financially despite his artistic successes.
key_figures: Lorenzo Da Ponte (librettist of Don Giovanni)

sources.name: Mozart's thematic catalogue (Verzeichnüß aller meiner Werke), autograph entry
Mozart's correspondence
Maynard Solomon, Mozart: A Life (1995)

events.certainty_note: Mozart's own thematic catalogue provides exact completion dates. Don Giovanni premiere documented in Prague press.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's military campaigns in Taiwan
events.description: In 1787, a major rebellion led by Lin Shuangwen erupted in Taiwan against Qing authority. Lin, a leader of the Tiandihui (Heaven and Earth Society), rallied followers among discontented Han Chinese settlers and captured several towns including Changhua and Zhuluo. The Qing court dispatched a large military force under Fukang'an, which suppressed the rebellion by early 1788. The Qianlong Emperor counted the suppression among his 'Ten Great Campaigns.' The rebellion highlighted ongoing tensions between settlers and the Qing administration and between different settler communities on the island.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing dynasty)
John Shepherd, Statecraft and Political Economy on the Taiwan Frontier 1600–1800 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Qing imperial records and local gazetteers.

events.title: Société des Amis des Noirs founded in Paris
events.description: On 19 February 1787, Jacques-Pierre Brissot and Étienne Clavière founded the Société des Amis des Noirs (Society of the Friends of the Blacks) in Paris, modeled on the British abolitionist movement. The society advocated for the gradual abolition of the slave trade and improvements in the condition of enslaved people in the French colonies. Members included Condorcet, Lafayette, Mirabeau, and the Abbé Grégoire. While its immediate impact was limited, the society played an important role in bringing abolitionist ideas into French revolutionary politics.

sources.name: Société des Amis des Noirs, minutes and correspondence (Archives nationales, Paris)
Marcel Dorigny and Bernard Gainot, La Société des Amis des Noirs 1788–1799 (1998)

events.certainty_note: Founding date is documented in society records. Some sources date formal establishment to February 1788 when public meetings began, but organizational efforts began in 1787.

events.title: Marla uprising in the Maratha Confederacy
events.description: The Maratha Confederacy in 1787 was wracked by internal power struggles among its major chiefs. Peshwa Madhavrao II was a figurehead, with real power contested between Mahadji Shinde (Scindia) in the north and Nana Fadnavis at Pune. Tipu Sultan's aggression from the south and growing British influence further complicated the political landscape. These internal divisions prevented the Marathas from presenting a unified front and would ultimately contribute to their defeat in the Anglo-Maratha Wars of the following decades.
key_figures: Mahadji Shinde (Scindia, de facto northern power)

sources.name: Marathi-language contemporary correspondence (Peshwa Daftar records)
Stewart Gordon, The Marathas 1600–1818 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Maratha administrative records and British East India Company correspondence, though internal Maratha politics of this period are complex and some details are debated.

events.title: Freed slave Ottobah Cugoano publishes Thoughts and Sentiments
events.description: In 1787, Ottobah Cugoano, a formerly enslaved Fante man from present-day Ghana, published Thoughts and Sentiments on the Evil and Wicked Traffic of the Slavery and Commerce of the Human Species in London. The work was one of the first published attacks on slavery by a formerly enslaved person in English and went further than many white abolitionists of the time, calling for immediate and total abolition rather than gradual reform. Cugoano was associated with the Sons of Africa, a group of formerly enslaved Africans active in the British abolitionist movement.

sources.name: Ottobah Cugoano, Thoughts and Sentiments on the Evil and Wicked Traffic of the Slavery and Commerce of the Human Species (1787)
Vincent Carretta, ed., introduction to Thoughts and Sentiments (Penguin edition, 1999)

events.certainty_note: The published work survives. Some scholars debate the extent of editorial assistance Cugoano may have received.

events.title: Siamese-Burmese conflict and consolidation under Rama I
events.description: King Rama I (Phra Phutthayotfa Chulalok) of the Chakri dynasty continued consolidating the new Siamese kingdom from its capital at Bangkok, established in 1782. The kingdom remained in periodic conflict with Burma (Konbaung dynasty) and was expanding its influence over Laos, Cambodia, and the northern Tai principalities. Rama I also sponsored a major revision of Siamese legal codes and Buddhist texts during this period, reinforcing the kingdom's cultural and religious identity after the destruction of Ayutthaya in 1767.

sources.name: Royal Chronicles of Bangkok (Phraratchaphongsawadan Krung Rattanakosin)
B.J. Terwiel, Thailand's Political History (2005)

events.certainty_note: Thai royal chronicles are the primary source; specific events for individual years in the 1780s can be difficult to date precisely.

events.title: Flour War crisis and bread riots in France
events.description: Throughout 1787, France experienced severe economic distress exacerbated by the disastrous Eden Treaty of 1786 with Britain, which had flooded French markets with cheap British manufactured goods, devastating the textile industry. Unemployment rose sharply, and bread prices increased. Combined with the fiscal crisis of the crown, these economic hardships created widespread popular discontent that would feed into the revolutionary ferment of 1788–1789. The economic dimension of the pre-revolutionary crisis is often overshadowed by the political narrative of the Assembly of Notables and parlementary resistance.

sources.name: Contemporary economic reports and intendants' correspondence (Archives nationales)
Ernest Labrousse, La Crise de l'économie française à la fin de l'Ancien Régime (1944)

events.certainty_note: Economic data from intendants' reports and price records is well preserved for this period.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly repeated claims about 1787 require qualification: (1) The 'Potemkin village' story — that Grigory Potemkin constructed elaborate fake villages to deceive Catherine II during her Crimean tour — is likely exaggerated or apocryphal. Modern historians (including Simon Sebag Montefiore and Alexander Panchenko) argue the story was largely invented by Georg von Helbig, a Saxon diplomat hostile to Potemkin, and amplified by rivals. Potemkin did embellish displays but did not construct entirely fake settlements. (2) The founding date of the Société des Amis des Noirs is contested: some historians place the formal establishment in February 1788, with 1787 representing preliminary organizational meetings. (3) The Constitutional Convention is sometimes portrayed as a consensus-driven process, but it was marked by intense conflict, walkouts (Luther Martin, Robert Yates, and John Lansing left before signing), and refusals to sign by prominent delegates including Edmund Randolph, George Mason, and Elbridge Gerry. The Constitution was deeply controversial at the time and ratification was not assured. (4) The Three-Fifths Compromise is sometimes attributed solely to the 1787 Convention, but the three-fifths ratio was first proposed in a 1783 amendment to the Articles of Confederation for taxation purposes; the Convention repurposed it for representation.
historiographic_note: The year 1787 is extremely well-documented for Europe and North America, with abundant primary sources including government records, personal correspondence, published works, and diplomatic archives. Coverage becomes thinner for South and Southeast Asia, where European sources provide an external perspective while indigenous records (Maratha daftars, Siamese chronicles, Qing archives) offer internal views that have been increasingly integrated by modern scholarship. Sub-Saharan African history for this period relies heavily on European trading records and abolitionist accounts, which carry inherent biases. The overwhelming dominance of the US Constitutional Convention in English-language historiography creates a risk of treating 1787 as primarily an American story, when in fact the pre-revolutionary crisis in France, the Russo-Turkish War, and events across Asia were of equal or greater global significance at the time.

graph_edges.note: The Constitution drafted at the Convention was sent to states for ratification, completed in 1788
Failure of the Assembly of Notables deepened the fiscal and political crisis that triggered the Revolution
The Russo-Turkish War begun in 1787 concluded with the Treaty of Jassy in 1792
Catherine's provocative Crimean tour contributed to Ottoman decision to declare war
Exiled Dutch Patriots in France became supporters and participants in the French Revolution
First Fleet's departure led to the founding of the British penal colony at Sydney Cove
Shays' Rebellion alarmed political elites and strengthened the case for a stronger central government
Qing military response escalated into full suppression campaign completed in 1788
The Three-Fifths Compromise was superseded by the Fourteenth Amendment after the Civil War
Economic crisis and political crisis reinforced each other in pre-revolutionary France

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The late 1780s sat at the hinge between the Enlightenment and the Age of Revolutions. In Europe, absolutist monarchies faced mounting fiscal crises and reform pressures — France most acutely. The newly independent United States struggled under the Articles of Confederation. The Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor remained powerful but was showing internal strains. The Ottoman Empire pursued selective modernisation under Abdülhamid I. Britain's East India Company was rapidly expanding its territorial control across the Indian subcontinent, while the Atlantic slave trade continued at enormous scale.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading records
Aboriginal Australian communities prior to sustained European contact
Pacific Island societies beyond initial European exploration accounts
Central Asian steppe polities
Interior Southeast Asian kingdoms beyond court chronicles

events.title: Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom enacted
events.description: On 16 January 1786, the Virginia General Assembly passed the Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom, drafted by Thomas Jefferson in 1777 and shepherded through the legislature by James Madison. The statute disestablished the Anglican Church in Virginia and prohibited compulsion in matters of religion, declaring that civil rights had no dependence on religious opinions. It became a foundational precedent for the First Amendment to the U.S. Constitution and was regarded by Jefferson as one of his three greatest achievements.
key_figures: Thomas Jefferson (author, then Minister to France)

sources.name: Virginia Statute for Religious Freedom, Acts of the Virginia General Assembly
Thomas Jefferson, Autobiography (1821)
Merrill Peterson, Thomas Jefferson and the New Nation (1970)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary legislative record survives; exact date of passage is documented.
cross_references: 1791-001 (ratification of the Bill of Rights including the First Amendment)

events.title: Premiere of Mozart's The Marriage of Figaro
events.description: On 1 May 1786, Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart's opera Le nozze di Figaro premiered at the Burgtheater in Vienna, with libretto by Lorenzo Da Ponte adapted from Beaumarchais's controversial play. The opera subverted aristocratic conventions by portraying servants outsmarting their master, resonating with Enlightenment critiques of privilege. Its initial Vienna run was modest, but a triumphant Prague reception later in 1786 cemented Mozart's reputation and led directly to the commission for Don Giovanni. It is now regarded as one of the supreme achievements of Western opera.
key_figures: Emperor Joseph II (patron, permitted the performance)

sources.name: Wiener Zeitung, contemporary press notices (May 1786)
Mozart's letters to Leopold Mozart
H.C. Robbins Landon, Mozart: The Golden Years (1989)

events.certainty_note: Premiere date confirmed by contemporary Viennese sources and Mozart's correspondence.

events.title: Death of Frederick the Great of Prussia
events.description: Frederick II of Prussia died at Sanssouci Palace on 17 August 1786 after a 46-year reign that transformed Prussia into a major European power. His military campaigns, administrative reforms, and patronage of Enlightenment thinkers defined an era of Prussian expansion and modernisation. He was succeeded by his nephew Frederick William II, whose more conservative and mystically inclined rule reversed many of Frederick's policies. Frederick's legacy shaped Prussian and German statecraft for the next century and a half.

sources.name: Contemporary diplomatic dispatches from European courts
Tim Blanning, Frederick the Great: King of Prussia (2015)

events.certainty_note: Death date and circumstances well documented by multiple contemporary sources.

events.title: First ascent of Mont Blanc
events.region: Chamonix, Duchy of Savoy (Kingdom of Sardinia)
events.description: On 8 August 1786, Jacques Balmat, a crystal hunter and chamois hunter, and Michel-Gabriel Paccard, a physician, completed the first ascent of Mont Blanc (4,808 m), the highest peak in the Alps. The climb was motivated by a prize offered by Horace Bénédict de Saussure, a Geneva naturalist, who had offered a reward for the first ascent in 1760. The achievement is conventionally regarded as the birth of modern mountaineering and stimulated scientific interest in high-altitude meteorology and geology.
key_figures: Horace Bénédict de Saussure (naturalist, prize sponsor)

sources.name: Paccard's journal and Saussure's correspondence
Marc-Théodore Bourrit, contemporary accounts (partially disputed)
Peter H. Hansen, The Summits of Modern Man (2013)

events.certainty_note: Date confirmed by multiple witnesses in Chamonix. Credit controversy exists (see disconfirming_evidence).

events.title: Shays' Rebellion begins in Massachusetts
events.description: Beginning in August 1786, armed farmers in western Massachusetts, led by Revolutionary War veteran Daniel Shays, forcibly shut down county courts to prevent debt collection and farm foreclosures. The rebellion arose from a post-war economic depression, deflationary monetary policy, and heavy state taxation. State militia under General Benjamin Lincoln eventually suppressed the revolt in early 1787, but the insurrection profoundly alarmed national leaders and became a decisive argument for strengthening the federal government, directly catalysing the Constitutional Convention of 1787.
key_figures: Daniel Shays (rebellion leader, former Continental Army captain)

sources.name: Massachusetts state government records and correspondence of George Washington, Henry Knox, and others
Leonard L. Richards, Shays's Rebellion: The American Revolution's Final Battle (2002)
