The manifesto offered generous terms: religious freedom, exemption from military service, tax holidays, self-governance, and free land. The policy attracted tens of thousands of settlers, predominantly German-speaking, who established communities along the Volga and in southern Russia. These 'Volga Germans' maintained distinct cultural identities for generations. The manifesto reflected Enlightenment-era populationist economic theory and Catherine's desire to develop productive agriculture in underpopulated frontier regions.
key_figures: Count Grigory Orlov (advisor and supporter of colonisation policy)

sources.name: Manifesto of 22 July 1763, Complete Collection of Laws of the Russian Empire
Roger Bartlett, Human Capital: The Settlement of Foreigners in Russia, 1762–1804 (1979)

events.certainty_note: The manifesto text survives in full in the official legal compilation.

events.title: Mason–Dixon survey begins
events.description: In late 1763, English astronomers Charles Mason and Jeremiah Dixon arrived in Philadelphia to begin the survey of the disputed boundary between the colonies of Maryland and Pennsylvania. The boundary dispute between the Calvert (Maryland) and Penn (Pennsylvania) families had festered since the 1680s. Mason and Dixon employed the most precise astronomical instruments of the era, including a zenith sector by John Bird, to establish the line at 39°43'26" N latitude. The survey, completed in 1767, became one of the most celebrated feats of colonial-era surveying and later became symbolically associated with the division between slave and free states in the United States.
key_figures: Thomas Penn and Richard Penn (Pennsylvania proprietors)

sources.name: Mason and Dixon's journal (manuscript, held at the National Archives, UK)
Edwin Danson, Drawing the Line: How Mason and Dixon Surveyed the Most Famous Border in America (2001)

events.certainty_note: Mason and Dixon's detailed journal survives. Their arrival date and initial observations are precisely recorded.

events.title: Expulsion of Jesuits from France
events.description: In 1763, the French parlements enforced the suppression of the Jesuit order that had been building since 1761, when the Parlement of Paris declared the Jesuit constitutions incompatible with French law. By 1763, Jesuits throughout France were required to renounce their vows or face exile. The suppression was driven by a combination of Gallican hostility to ultramontane papal authority, Enlightenment anti-clericalism, and the financial scandal of Père Antoine Lavalette's bankrupt Caribbean trading venture. France followed Portugal (1759) in expelling the order, and Spain would follow in 1767, building pressure that led Pope Clement XIV to suppress the entire order globally in 1773.
key_figures: Duke of Choiseul (minister who facilitated suppression)
Madame de Pompadour (influential supporter of suppression)

sources.name: Arrêts of the Parlement of Paris, 1761–1764
Dale Van Kley, The Jansenists and the Expulsion of the Jesuits from France, 1757–1765 (1975)

events.certainty_note: Legal records of the parlements and extensive pamphlet literature survive.

events.title: Toussaint Louverture born
events.description: François-Dominique Toussaint Louverture, the future leader of the Haitian Revolution and the most successful slave revolt in history, was born on the Bréda plantation in Saint-Domingue around 1743, though the traditional date of 20 May 1743 is uncertain. Some scholars have argued for a birth date as late as 1763 based on alternative readings of limited sources, but the earlier date is now more widely accepted. Regardless of exact birth year, Toussaint was born into slavery on a French Caribbean sugar plantation, the son of an enslaved man reportedly of Arada (Allada, in modern Benin) origin. He would become one of the most significant figures of the Age of Revolution.

sources.name: Philippe Girard, Toussaint Louverture: A Revolutionary Life (2016) — argues for c. 1743 birth date
Plantation records from Bréda estate (fragmentary)

events.certainty_note: Birth date is debated among scholars. The traditional 1743 date is now preferred over the once-accepted 1763 date, but uncertainty remains due to limited records for enslaved persons.

events.title: Burma–Siam conflict intensifies under Hsinbyushin
events.description: In 1763, Hsinbyushin succeeded his father Naungdawgyi as king of Burma's Konbaung dynasty. Hsinbyushin was an aggressive expansionist who inherited a reunified Burma and immediately began planning campaigns against Siam (the Ayutthaya kingdom), the Manipur states, and eventually Qing China. Under his direction, Burmese forces began the military preparations and border campaigns that would culminate in the devastating siege and destruction of Ayutthaya in 1767. The Burmese–Siamese conflicts of this decade were among the largest military operations in Southeast Asian history and resulted in the complete destruction of the Ayutthaya kingdom, one of the most prosperous polities in the region.

sources.name: Glass Palace Chronicle (Hmannan Yazawin), compiled 1829 from earlier court records
Victor Lieberman, Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context, c. 800–1830 (2003/2009)

events.certainty_note: Succession date is well established in Burmese royal chronicles and corroborated by foreign sources.

events.title: Spain reforms colonial administration under Charles III
events.description: In the aftermath of the Seven Years' War, during which Spain lost Havana and Manila to the British (recovered via the Treaty of Paris in exchange for Florida), Charles III accelerated the Bourbon Reforms to modernise and centralise the Spanish colonial empire. In 1763, he appointed the reformist minister José de Gálvez and initiated military and fiscal restructuring of New Spain and other colonies. The reforms included strengthening colonial defences, reorganising tax collection, and reducing creole autonomy in favour of peninsular Spanish administrators. These reforms increased imperial revenue but bred resentment among colonial elites that would contribute to independence movements in the early nineteenth century.
key_figures: Count of Aranda (President of the Council of Castile)
Marquis of Esquilache (minister of finance and war)

sources.name: Spanish royal decrees and correspondence, Archivo General de Indias
John Lynch, Bourbon Spain, 1700–1808 (1989)

events.certainty_note: Reforms are extensively documented in Spanish state archives. Some specific reform dates extend into 1764–1765.

events.title: Bayes' theorem published posthumously
events.description: The Reverend Thomas Bayes' 'An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances' was read posthumously before the Royal Society on 23 December 1763, communicated by Richard Price. Bayes, a Presbyterian minister and mathematician who had died in 1761, formulated the first mathematical expression of inverse probability — the foundation of what is now known as Bayesian statistics. Richard Price edited the manuscript, added a significant introduction, and supplied applications. The theorem, which provides a method for updating probability estimates as new evidence emerges, would eventually become foundational to modern statistics, machine learning, and decision theory, though its significance was not widely appreciated until the twentieth century.
key_figures: Richard Price (editor and communicator of the paper)

sources.name: Thomas Bayes, 'An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances,' Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, vol. 53 (1763)
Sharon Bertsch McGrayne, The Theory That Would Not Die: How Bayes' Rule Cracked the Enigma Code, Hunted Down Russian Submarines, and Emerged Triumphant from Two Centuries of Controversy (2011)

events.certainty_note: The published paper in the Philosophical Transactions is precisely dated. Bayes' death in 1761 is documented in parish records.

events.title: John Harrison's H4 marine chronometer tested
events.description: In 1763, the Board of Longitude evaluated the results of John Harrison's H4 marine chronometer, which had been tested on a voyage to Jamaica in 1761–1762. The watch had determined longitude with an error of only 1.25 minutes of arc, far exceeding the requirements for the £20,000 Longitude Prize established by Parliament in 1714. However, the Board, influenced by the Astronomer Royal Nevil Maskelyne who favoured the rival lunar distance method, refused to award the full prize, demanding further trials and insisting Harrison reveal the mechanism's construction. The dispute would drag on until 1773, when George III personally intervened. Harrison's chronometer represented a revolution in maritime navigation that would make oceanic travel vastly safer and more efficient.
key_figures: Nevil Maskelyne (Astronomer Royal, rival advocate of lunar distance method)
William Harrison (John's son, accompanied H4 on trial)

sources.name: Minutes of the Board of Longitude, Cambridge University Library
Dava Sobel, Longitude: The True Story of a Lone Genius Who Solved the Greatest Scientific Problem of His Time (1995)

events.certainty_note: Board of Longitude minutes and Harrison's own records survive in detail.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's Ten Great Campaigns continue
events.description: The Qianlong Emperor's reign (1735–1796) was at its zenith in 1763, with the Qing Empire at its greatest territorial extent following the conquest of the Dzungar Khanate (completed 1757) and the pacification of the Tarim Basin (1758–1759). By 1763, the Qing had consolidated control over Xinjiang ('New Frontier') and were administering it through a military-bureaucratic system. The emperor was also contending with ongoing frontier management challenges in Tibet, Mongolia, and the southwest, while presiding over one of the largest and most prosperous empires in world history, with a population estimated at approximately 200 million. Court culture flourished, with the continuation of the massive Siku Quanshu (Complete Library of the Four Treasuries) literary compilation project beginning in 1773.
key_figures: Qianlong Emperor (Aisin Gioro Hongli, Emperor of China)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Peter Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia (2005)

events.certainty_note: Qing imperial records for this period are extraordinarily detailed.

events.title: George Grenville becomes Prime Minister of Great Britain
events.description: On 16 April 1763, George Grenville succeeded the Earl of Bute as Prime Minister, beginning a ministry that would profoundly affect British-colonial relations. Grenville, a fiscal conservative, immediately turned his attention to the enormous national debt accumulated during the Seven Years' War (approximately £130 million). His conviction that American colonists should contribute to the cost of their own defence led directly to the Sugar Act (1764) and the Stamp Act (1765), which provoked the first major colonial resistance to parliamentary taxation. Grenville's ministry thus inaugurated the twelve-year political crisis that culminated in the American Revolution.

sources.name: The Grenville Papers, ed. William James Smith (1852–1853)
Philip Lawson, George Grenville: A Political Life (1984)

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated in parliamentary records and royal correspondence.

events.title: Wilkes affair and North Briton No. 45
events.description: On 23 April 1763, John Wilkes published issue No. 45 of The North Briton, attacking the King's Speech (written by ministers) as containing falsehoods about the Treaty of Paris. The government responded with a general warrant for the arrest of all persons connected with the publication. Wilkes was arrested on 30 April, but the Chief Justice of the Common Pleas, Charles Pratt (later Lord Camden), released him on the grounds of parliamentary privilege. The case became a landmark in British civil liberties: general warrants were subsequently declared illegal, and Wilkes became a hero of the radical reform movement. The 'Wilkes and Liberty' agitation would continue for over a decade and influenced American colonial conceptions of press freedom and the rights of the subject.
key_figures: Earl of Halifax (Secretary of State, issued the warrant)
Chief Justice Charles Pratt (ruled for Wilkes)
Earl of Sandwich (former ally who turned against Wilkes)

sources.name: The North Briton, No. 45, 23 April 1763
Arthur H. Cash, John Wilkes: The Scandalous Father of Civil Liberty (2006)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in court records, parliamentary proceedings, and the London press.

events.title: End of the French and Indian War in North America
events.description: With the Treaty of Paris, France formally surrendered its North American possessions and the French and Indian War — the North American theatre of the Seven Years' War — came to its official close. British forces had occupied Montreal since 1760, but the formal transfer of sovereignty in 1763 brought massive geopolitical change: French-speaking populations in Canada (approximately 65,000) came under British rule, French-allied Indigenous nations lost their ability to play European powers against each other (the 'middle ground' strategy), and the entire continent east of the Mississippi became British territory. The removal of the French threat paradoxically weakened the bond between Britain and its American colonies by eliminating the principal reason colonists had needed British military protection.
key_figures: General Thomas Gage (military governor of Montreal)

sources.name: Articles of Capitulation of Montreal (1760) and Treaty of Paris (1763)
Richard White, The Middle Ground: Indians, Empires, and Republics in the Great Lakes Region, 1650–1815 (1991)

events.certainty_note: Formal transfer documented in treaty text and subsequent administrative records.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly held beliefs about 1763 require qualification: (1) The smallpox blanket incident at Fort Pitt (1763-006) is often presented as evidence of a systematic British policy of biological warfare. While the documentary evidence of intent is clear (Amherst's letters and Trent's journal), there is no conclusive epidemiological evidence that the specific blankets distributed caused an outbreak. Smallpox was already present in the region, and the attribution of any particular outbreak to this incident is unproven. Elizabeth Fenn's scholarship argues the intent is historically significant regardless of effectiveness, while Philip Ranlet has questioned whether the blankets could have transmitted the virus. (2) Pontiac's War (1763-004) was long framed as 'Pontiac's Conspiracy' following Francis Parkman's influential 1851 account, which portrayed Pontiac as the mastermind of a centrally coordinated plot. Modern scholarship (particularly Gregory Evans Dowd and Richard White) has shown that the uprising was decentralised, with multiple Indigenous leaders acting semi-independently based on shared grievances rather than under Pontiac's command. (3) The traditional birth date of Toussaint Louverture (1763-013) has been revised by most modern scholars to c. 1743, though the older 1763 date still circulates in popular sources. (4) The Royal Proclamation of 1763 (1763-003) is sometimes described as a benevolent British recognition of Indigenous sovereignty. While it did acknowledge Native land rights, its primary motivation was pragmatic: reducing the cost of frontier warfare and maintaining orderly settlement for British benefit, not protecting Indigenous autonomy as an end in itself.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1763 is exceptionally rich for Europe and British North America, with abundant primary sources including diplomatic correspondence, military dispatches, parliamentary records, newspapers, and personal journals. However, this very richness creates a bias: events are disproportionately seen through British imperial lenses. Indigenous perspectives on Pontiac's War must be reconstructed from hostile or paternalistic European accounts and later oral traditions. French-Canadian voices are similarly filtered through British administrative records. For South and Southeast Asia, European trading company records provide detailed but commercially biased documentation, while indigenous court chronicles (such as Burmese royal records) present their own court-centred perspective. Sub-Saharan African and Pacific histories for this year are largely absent from the European documentary record, which does not mean nothing significant occurred — it means the surviving sources do not capture it.

graph_edges.note: Treaty of Paris concluded the global conflict begun in 1756
Removal of French threat and subsequent British taxation policies contributed to colonial rebellion
The Royal Proclamation was partly a response to Pontiac's War
Pontiac's War demonstrated the need for a boundary line policy
Mir Qasim's conflict with the East India Company led directly to Buxar
French expulsion was part of the cascade leading to global Jesuit suppression
Hsinbyushin's accession set Burma on a path toward destroying Ayutthaya
Foundational paper for Bayesian inference and modern statistical reasoning
Grenville's fiscal policies led directly to the Stamp Act crisis
The North Briton affair launched a decade of radical agitation for civil liberties
Prussia's confirmed great-power status after Hubertusburg enabled Frederick's role in partitioning Poland

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The year 1762 falls near the end of the Seven Years' War (1756–1763), the first truly global conflict, fought across Europe, the Americas, West Africa, India, and the Philippines. In Europe, Frederick the Great of Prussia was saved from annihilation by the death of Empress Elizabeth of Russia and the accession of the pro-Prussian Peter III — the so-called 'Miracle of the House of Brandenburg.' Britain, under the elder Pitt's strategic vision, was completing its conquest of French and Spanish colonial possessions. The Enlightenment was at its intellectual apex, with Rousseau publishing two of the most influential works of modern political thought. In East Asia, the Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor was at the height of its power, while in Southeast Asia the Burmese Konbaung dynasty was waging aggressive wars of expansion.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Aboriginal Australian societies
Pacific Island communities
Interior South American indigenous societies
Central Asian khanates beyond Qing frontier records

events.title: Death of Empress Elizabeth; Peter III becomes Tsar
events.description: Empress Elizabeth of Russia died on 5 January 1762 (25 December 1761 Old Style). Her nephew succeeded as Peter III. His accession immediately transformed European geopolitics: Peter, an ardent admirer of Frederick the Great, moved to end Russia's participation in the Seven Years' War. This single dynastic transition reversed Russia's military position from Prussia's most dangerous enemy to its ally, fundamentally altering the war's outcome and saving Frederick from probable defeat.

sources.name: Court records and diplomatic dispatches of the Russian Imperial Court
Isabel de Madariaga, Russia in the Age of Catherine the Great (1981)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Russian court records, foreign ambassadors' dispatches, and contemporary correspondence.

events.title: Treaty of Saint Petersburg: Russia-Prussia Peace
events.description: On 5 May 1762 (24 April OS), Peter III concluded peace with Prussia through the Treaty of Saint Petersburg, returning all territories Russia had conquered during the Seven Years' War, including East Prussia. Russia received nothing in return. The treaty was followed by an alliance on 19 June. This diplomatic revolution — returning conquered territory without compensation — was deeply unpopular among Russian military officers and court elites, contributing directly to Peter's overthrow weeks later. For Frederick the Great, it was a miraculous salvation from near-certain defeat.

sources.name: Treaty text in Russian and Prussian diplomatic archives
Christopher Clark, Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia, 1600–1947 (2006)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives; extensively documented in diplomatic correspondence from multiple nations.

events.title: Coup d'état of Catherine the Great
events.description: On 9 July 1762 (28 June OS), Catherine, wife of Peter III, seized the throne in a palace coup backed by the Imperial Guard regiments, particularly the Izmailovsky, Semenovsky, and Preobrazhensky Guards. Peter III abdicated and was killed on 17 July under murky circumstances while in custody at Ropsha, likely with the involvement of Alexei Orlov. Catherine's accession inaugurated a 34-year reign that would profoundly reshape Russia, expanding the empire, reforming administration, patronizing the Enlightenment, and entrenching serfdom. She is the longest-ruling female leader in Russian history.
key_figures: Peter III (Emperor of Russia, deposed and killed)
Alexei Orlov (implicated in Peter III's death)

sources.name: Catherine II, Memoirs (written later but describing events she participated in)
Diplomatic dispatches of foreign ambassadors in St. Petersburg, especially the French and British envoys
Robert K. Massie, Catherine the Great: Portrait of a Woman (2011)

events.certainty_note: Abundantly documented; the exact circumstances of Peter III's death remain debated, but the coup itself is uncontested.

events.title: British Capture of Martinique
events.description: On 16 February 1762, French Martinique surrendered to a British amphibious force under Admiral George Rodney and General Robert Monckton after a campaign begun in January. Martinique was the most valuable French sugar island in the Caribbean and a base for French privateers. Its capture was followed by the rapid British seizure of St. Lucia, Grenada, and St. Vincent. These conquests gave Britain almost total control of the Caribbean and became major bargaining chips in the peace negotiations that ended the Seven Years' War.
key_figures: Le Vassor de La Touche (French governor of Martinique)

sources.name: Admiralty and War Office records, The National Archives, Kew
Richard Harding, The Emergence of Britain's Global Naval Supremacy (2010)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in British military and naval records.

events.title: Spain Enters the Seven Years' War; British Declare War on Spain
events.description: Following the Third Family Compact between the Bourbon monarchies of France and Spain (signed August 1761), Spain formally entered the Seven Years' War against Britain. Britain declared war on Spain on 4 January 1762. Spain's entry widened the war significantly, bringing Spanish colonial territories into play as targets for British naval and amphibious operations. However, Spain's military and naval forces were unprepared, and its entry came too late to reverse the tide; instead, it exposed Spanish colonies — particularly Havana and Manila — to devastating British attacks.
key_figures: William Pitt the Elder (had advocated pre-emptive war on Spain)

sources.name: Diplomatic correspondence in the Spanish and British archives
Jonathan Dull, The French Navy and the Seven Years' War (2005)

events.certainty_note: Formal declarations of war are a matter of public record.

events.title: British Siege and Capture of Havana
events.description: From June to August 1762, a massive British expedition besieged and captured Havana, the key to Spain's Caribbean empire and the assembly point for the annual treasure fleet. The British force of over 200 ships and 14,000 troops, commanded by the Earl of Albemarle and Admiral Sir George Pocock, took the fortress of El Morro on 30 July and the city surrendered on 13 August. The capture yielded enormous prize money and temporarily gave Britain control of Cuba's sugar wealth. Havana was returned to Spain in the 1763 Treaty of Paris in exchange for Florida, reshaping the map of North America.
key_figures: George Keppel, 3rd Earl of Albemarle (British commander)
Admiral Sir George Pocock (British naval commander)
Juan de Prado (Spanish Governor of Cuba)
Luis de Velasco (heroic Spanish defender of El Morro, killed in action)

sources.name: Admiralty records and campaign journals, The National Archives, Kew
Elena Schneider, The Occupation of Havana: War, Trade, and Slavery in the Atlantic World (2018)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in British, Spanish, and Cuban sources.

events.title: British Capture of Manila
events.description: On 6 October 1762, British forces under Brigadier General William Draper and Admiral Samuel Cornish captured Manila after a brief siege, catching the Spanish colonial administration almost completely unprepared. The capture of Manila — on the opposite side of the globe from the main theatre of war — demonstrated the global reach of British naval power. However, Spanish resistance continued in the countryside under Simón de Anda y Salazar, who organized Filipino and Spanish forces and maintained a rival government. Manila was returned to Spain by the Treaty of Paris in 1763.
key_figures: Archbishop Manuel Rojo del Río (acting Spanish Governor-General)
Simón de Anda y Salazar (organised continued Spanish resistance)

sources.name: British East India Company records and Admiralty dispatches
Nicholas Tracy, Manila Ransomed: The British Assault on Manila in the Seven Years War (1995)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in British, Spanish, and Philippine colonial records.

events.title: Publication of Rousseau's The Social Contract
events.description: Jean-Jacques Rousseau's Du contrat social (The Social Contract) was published in April 1762 in Amsterdam. Opening with the famous declaration 'Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains,' the work argued that legitimate political authority rests on a social contract among citizens expressing the 'general will.' Though initially banned in both France and Geneva, the work became profoundly influential in shaping republican and democratic political theory, directly inspiring leaders of the French Revolution and influencing the development of modern political philosophy.

sources.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Du contrat social; ou Principes du droit politique (1762)
Robert Wokler, Rousseau: A Very Short Introduction (2001)

events.certainty_note: Publication date and text are unambiguous.

events.title: Publication of Rousseau's Émile and Its Condemnation
events.description: Rousseau's Émile, ou De l'éducation was published in May 1762. A treatise on education in the form of a novel, it proposed that children should learn through direct experience and natural development rather than rote instruction. The book's religious section, 'Profession of Faith of the Savoyard Vicar,' which argued for natural religion over revealed dogma, provoked immediate condemnation. The Paris Parlement ordered the book burned on 9 June and issued a warrant for Rousseau's arrest. Geneva similarly condemned both Émile and The Social Contract. Rousseau fled to Swiss territory and spent years in exile.
key_figures: Christophe de Beaumont (Archbishop of Paris, condemned Émile)

sources.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau, Émile, ou De l'éducation (1762)
Records of the Parlement de Paris, arrêt of 9 June 1762
Leo Damrosch, Jean-Jacques Rousseau: Restless Genius (2005)

events.certainty_note: Publication, condemnation, and Rousseau's flight are all thoroughly documented.

events.title: Battle of Freiberg — Last Major Battle of the Seven Years' War in Europe
events.description: On 29 October 1762, Prince Henry of Prussia defeated an Austrian force under General von Hadik at the Battle of Freiberg in Saxony. This was the last significant battle of the Seven Years' War in the European theatre. The Prussian victory, though relatively small in scale, secured Frederick the Great's hold on Saxony and strengthened Prussia's negotiating position in the peace talks that would produce the Treaty of Hubertusburg in February 1763. Prince Henry's competent generalship in this and other campaigns earned him considerable military reputation.

sources.name: Prussian and Austrian military campaign records
Franz Szabo, The Seven Years War in Europe, 1756–1763 (2008)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in military records of both sides.

events.title: Peter III's Manifesto on the Liberty of the Nobility
events.description: On 18 February 1762 (OS), Peter III issued the Manifesto on the Freedom of the Nobility (Манифест о вольности дворянства), abolishing the compulsory state service requirement for Russian nobles that had been in place since Peter the Great's era. Nobles were now free to resign from military or civil service at will, travel abroad, and enter foreign service. This was one of the most consequential social legislation acts in Russian history, fundamentally transforming the relationship between the nobility and the autocracy. Catherine II confirmed and expanded these privileges in her 1785 Charter to the Nobility.

sources.name: Manifesto text in Polnoe sobranie zakonov Rossiiskoi imperii (Complete Collection of Laws of the Russian Empire)
Robert E. Jones, The Emancipation of the Russian Nobility, 1762–1785 (1973)

events.certainty_note: Legislative text survives in full.

events.title: Suppression of the Jesuits in France
events.description: On 6 August 1762, the Parlement de Paris issued an arrêt formally dissolving the Society of Jesus in France and closing its colleges and institutions. This followed several years of legal and political attacks on the Jesuits, triggered partly by the bankruptcy of Father Antoine Lavalette's commercial ventures in Martinique. The suppression reflected the broader power struggle between Gallican parlementaires, Enlightenment philosophes, and the Jesuit order. France's action was part of a wider European trend: Portugal had expelled the Jesuits in 1759, and Spain would follow in 1767, culminating in Pope Clement XIV's global suppression of the order in 1773.
key_figures: Étienne François, duc de Choiseul (leading minister, supported suppression)
Antoine Lavalette (Jesuit whose financial scandal triggered the crisis)

sources.name: Arrêt of the Parlement de Paris, 6 August 1762
Dale Van Kley, The Jansenists and the Expulsion of the Jesuits from France, 1757–1765 (1975)

events.certainty_note: Legal records and extensive pamphlet literature survive.

events.title: Spanish Invasion of Portugal
events.description: In May 1762, Spain and France invaded Portugal, Britain's oldest ally, in the so-called Fantastic War (Guerra Fantástica). Spanish forces initially made gains in the northern border regions, but a British expeditionary force under the Count of Lippe, combined with Portuguese regulars and militia, mounted an effective defence. The Anglo-Portuguese forces used scorched earth tactics and guerrilla-style resistance to stall the Franco-Spanish advance. By late 1762 the invasion had failed to achieve its objectives, and Portugal remained in the British alliance.
key_figures: Marquis of Sarria (Spanish commander, later replaced)
Count of Aranda (replaced Sarria as Spanish commander)

sources.name: Military correspondence in Portuguese and British archives
David Francis, Portugal, 1715–1808 (1985)

events.certainty_note: Well documented in military records from Portugal, Spain, Britain, and France.

events.title: End of the Anglo-Cherokee War
events.description: The Anglo-Cherokee War (1758–1761) concluded with a peace treaty signed in late 1761 and ratified in 1762. The conflict had devastated Cherokee towns in the southern Appalachians after British forces under Lieutenant Colonel James Grant destroyed fifteen Middle Cherokee towns and their crops in 1761. The peace terms forced the Cherokee to cede territory and accept a boundary line. The war significantly weakened Cherokee power in the region and demonstrated the vulnerability of Native American nations even when allied with European powers. It also foreshadowed the pattern of frontier warfare and dispossession that would intensify after 1763.
key_figures: Lieutenant Colonel James Grant (British military commander)

sources.name: Colonial records of South Carolina and Virginia
Tyler Boulware, Deconstructing the Cherokee Nation: Town, Region, and Nation among Eighteenth-Century Cherokees (2011)

events.certainty_note: Documented in colonial government records, though precise treaty dates are sometimes given as late 1761.

events.title: Burmese-Siamese War: Fall of Ayutthaya Campaign Begins
events.description: In 1762, the Konbaung dynasty of Burma under King Alaungpaya's successors intensified military pressure on the Ayutthaya Kingdom of Siam. Burmese forces began a major campaign that would culminate in the siege and destruction of the Siamese capital Ayutthaya in April 1767. The early phases in 1762–1764 involved Burmese advances through tributary states and border regions. This conflict was one of the largest and most destructive wars in Southeast Asian history, resulting in the complete destruction of Ayutthaya and a fundamental restructuring of Siamese polity under the subsequent Thonburi and Rattanakosin kingdoms.

sources.name: Hmannan Yazawin (Glass Palace Chronicle), compiled 1829 from earlier sources
Royal Autograph Chronicle of Ayutthaya
Victor Lieberman, Strange Parallels: Southeast Asia in Global Context (2003–2009)

events.certainty_note: The broader conflict is well established; precise dates of specific 1762 military operations vary between Burmese and Thai sources.

events.title: Qing Campaign Against Burmese Border Incursions
events.description: In 1762, tensions escalated on the Qing-Burmese frontier in Yunnan province as Burmese forces and allied Shan groups raided across the border. These incursions would eventually provoke the Sino-Burmese War of 1765–1769, one of the Qianlong Emperor's most costly and least successful military campaigns. In 1762, the Qing response was still largely handled by local officials and frontier garrison forces. The situation reflected the Konbaung dynasty's expansionism under multiple kings and the difficulty of projecting Qing power into the mountainous borderlands of Southeast Asia.

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Yingcong Dai, The Sichuan Frontier and Tibet: Imperial Strategy in the Early Qing (2009); and related studies on Qing-Burma frontier

events.certainty_note: Documented in Qing administrative records; the escalation timeline is reconstructed from several years of frontier reports.

events.title: British Capture of St. Lucia, Grenada, and St. Vincent
events.description: Following the capture of Martinique in February, British forces rapidly seized the remaining French Windward Islands. St. Lucia fell on 26 February, Grenada surrendered on 4 March, and St. Vincent was taken shortly after. These conquests completed Britain's domination of the eastern Caribbean and eliminated the last French naval and privateering bases in the region. The islands became important negotiating assets in the Treaty of Paris peace talks; Britain returned St. Lucia to France but retained Grenada, St. Vincent, and the Grenadines.

sources.name: British Admiralty and Colonial Office records
N. A. M. Rodger, The Command of the Ocean: A Naval History of Britain, 1649–1815 (2004)

events.certainty_note: Dates of surrender are recorded in official military dispatches.

events.title: Bute Becomes Prime Minister; Peace Negotiations Begin
events.description: Lord Bute, the favourite of George III, had become Prime Minister in May 1762 following the resignation of the Duke of Newcastle. Bute was committed to ending the expensive Seven Years' War and began serious peace negotiations with France and Spain. His willingness to offer generous terms provoked fierce opposition, particularly from William Pitt and the City of London, who believed Britain should extract maximum advantage from its military victories. Bute's peace policy nonetheless prevailed, laying the groundwork for the Treaty of Paris signed in February 1763.
key_figures: John Stuart, 3rd Earl of Bute (Prime Minister)
Duke of Newcastle (resigned as Prime Minister)
Duc de Choiseul (French foreign minister, counterpart in negotiations)

sources.name: Parliamentary debates and Cabinet papers
Karl Schweizer, Frederick the Great, William Pitt, and Lord Bute: The Anglo-Prussian Alliance, 1756–1763 (1991)

events.certainty_note: Cabinet records and diplomatic correspondence survive extensively.

events.title: Diego de Silang's Revolt in the Philippines
events.description: Taking advantage of the British capture of Manila, Diego Silang led a revolt against Spanish colonial rule in the Ilocos region of northern Luzon in late 1762. Silang allied with the British, who appointed him as their representative in the region. He established a local government in Vigan and challenged both Spanish authority and the power of the religious orders. Though Silang was assassinated by Spanish-allied agents in May 1763, and his wife Gabriela continued the resistance before being captured and executed, the revolt represents one of the earliest Filipino uprisings against colonial rule and is commemorated in Philippine national memory.
key_figures: Gabriela Silang (continued the revolt after Diego's assassination)

sources.name: Spanish colonial administrative records from the Archivo General de Indias
William Henry Scott, The Discovery of the Igorots: Spanish Contacts with the Pagans of Northern Luzon (1974)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Spanish colonial records and later Philippine historical compilations.

events.title: Sweden-Prussia Peace (Treaty of Hamburg)
events.description: On 22 May 1762, Sweden and Prussia signed the Treaty of Hamburg, ending Swedish participation in the Seven Years' War. Sweden, which had entered the war against Prussia in 1757 as part of the anti-Prussian coalition, had conducted a desultory campaign in Pomerania with limited results. The treaty restored the pre-war status quo with no territorial changes. Sweden's ineffective performance in the war highlighted the declining military power of the once-dominant northern European kingdom.

sources.name: Treaty text in diplomatic records
Michael Roberts, The Age of Liberty: Sweden 1719–1772 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives; date is established.

events.title: Nizam Ali Khan Consolidates Power in Hyderabad
events.description: Nizam Ali Khan consolidated his position as the Nizam of Hyderabad after deposing and killing his brother Salabat Jang in 1762. Salabat Jang had been a French puppet during the Carnatic Wars, and his removal marked the definitive end of French influence in the Deccan. Nizam Ali Khan would rule Hyderabad for over four decades (until 1803), navigating the complex politics of Mughal decline, Maratha expansion, and the growing power of the British East India Company. His reign marked Hyderabad's transition from a theatre of European rivalry to a princely state accommodating British paramountcy.

sources.name: East India Company records, British Library
Yusuf Husain Khan, The First Nizam: The Life and Times of Nizam ul-Mulk Asaf Jah I (1963) and related Hyderabad historiography

events.certainty_note: Documented in British East India Company and Mughal court records, though the exact date of Salabat Jang's death varies slightly between sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The 'Miracle of the House of Brandenburg' — the idea that Elizabeth's death single-handedly saved Frederick from inevitable destruction — has been complicated by modern military historians who note that Prussia's strategic position, while dire, was not entirely hopeless even before January 1762, and that Austria's war fatigue was already advanced. The narrative of Catherine the Great's coup as a purely personal power grab has been nuanced by scholarship emphasizing the institutional role of the Guards regiments and the genuine policy grievances (especially Peter III's unpopular peace with Prussia and his Holstein-focused foreign policy) that motivated the conspirators. Regarding the British capture of Havana, the traditional emphasis on British military prowess has been balanced by scholarship noting that disease (particularly yellow fever) killed far more British soldiers than combat — roughly half the expedition's strength — raising questions about whether the operation was strategically sound despite its tactical success. The dating of the Anglo-Cherokee War's conclusion is sometimes placed in 1761 rather than 1762, depending on whether one counts the cessation of hostilities or the formal ratification of peace terms.
historiographic_note: The year 1762 is richly documented through European diplomatic archives, parliamentary records, military campaign journals, and published works. However, the surviving record is heavily skewed toward European state actors and their colonial operations. Indigenous perspectives on events like the Anglo-Cherokee War are reconstructed primarily from colonial records, which inevitably filter Native American agency through European categories. Southeast Asian events (Burma-Siam conflicts, Philippine revolts) are documented through a combination of colonial records and court chronicles compiled later, each with significant biases. The Burmese Glass Palace Chronicle and Siamese royal chronicles reflect court perspectives and were written decades after the events. Chinese frontier records focus narrowly on security threats as perceived from Beijing. The voices of ordinary people — soldiers, enslaved persons, peasants, indigenous communities — are largely absent from the record for this period across all regions.

graph_edges.note: Peter III's accession directly caused the Russia-Prussia peace treaty
Peter III's unpopular peace with Prussia was a major catalyst for Catherine's coup
Catherine's accession led to her reform program including the 1767 Nakaz
Spain's entry into the war exposed Havana to British attack
Spain's entry into the war exposed Manila to British attack
Havana's capture was exchanged for Florida in the peace settlement
The Social Contract's theory of popular sovereignty profoundly influenced revolutionary thought
Freiberg secured Prussia's negotiating position for the final peace
French suppression was part of the wider European campaign against the Jesuits
The 1762 campaign was an early phase of the war that destroyed Ayutthaya in 1767
The British capture of Manila enabled Silang's revolt in northern Luzon
Peter III's manifesto was confirmed and expanded by Catherine II's Charter

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The mid-eighteenth century is dominated by the Seven Years' War (1756–1763), the first truly global conflict, fought across Europe, the Americas, India, West Africa, and the Philippines. In South Asia, the Mughal Empire has fragmented into successor states competing with the expanding British East India Company and Afghan invaders. The European Enlightenment is in full flower, reshaping philosophy, science, and governance. Qing China under the Qianlong Emperor is at the height of its territorial extent and cultural production.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Interior Southeast Asia
Oceania and Pacific Islands
Central Asia beyond Afghan campaigns
Indigenous societies of the Americas outside European contact zones

events.title: Third Battle of Panipat
events.region: South Asia — Maratha Confederacy / Durrani Empire
events.description: On 14 January 1761, the forces of Ahmad Shah Durrani decisively defeated the Maratha Confederacy at Panipat, roughly 90 km north of Delhi. The Maratha army, led by Sadashivrao Bhau, was encircled after months of positional maneuvering and a supply blockade. Estimated casualties were enormous — possibly 40,000–70,000 Maratha combatants and camp followers killed. The defeat shattered Maratha ambitions to fill the Mughal power vacuum across northern India and created a political fragmentation that ultimately facilitated British East India Company expansion over the following decades. Ahmad Shah Durrani, however, was unable to consolidate his gains and withdrew to Afghanistan.
key_figures: Ahmad Shah Durrani (Shah of the Durrani Empire)
Vishwasrao (son of Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao, killed in battle)

sources.name: Tarikh-i-Ahmad Shahi by Mahmud al-Husayni
Kashiraj Pandit's account (Maratha court chronicle)
T.S. Shejwalkar, Panipat 1761 (1946)

events.certainty_note: Multiple contemporary accounts from both sides survive, including court chronicles and eyewitness letters. The date and outcome are not in dispute.

events.title: British capture of Pondicherry
events.region: South Asia — French India / British East India Company
events.description: On 15 January 1761 — just one day after Panipat — the French colonial capital of Pondicherry surrendered to British forces under Eyre Coote after a prolonged siege. This effectively ended French political and military power in India. The French governor Lally-Tollendal was later recalled to France and executed in 1766 for his failure. Though Pondicherry was returned to France at the 1763 Treaty of Paris, French influence in India never recovered, leaving Britain as the dominant European power on the subcontinent.
key_figures: Eyre Coote (British East India Company commander)
Thomas Arthur, Comte de Lally (French governor-general of India)

sources.name: East India Company records, British Library IOR series
G.B. Malleson, History of the French in India (1868)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in both British and French official records.

events.title: Battle of Villinghausen
events.description: On 15–16 July 1761, an Anglo-Hanoverian-Brunswick army under Duke Ferdinand of Brunswick defeated a larger French force under marshals Broglie and Soubise near Villinghausen in Westphalia. The victory prevented the French from overrunning Hanover and maintained the allied defensive position in western Germany during the Seven Years' War. It was one of Ferdinand's most skilful engagements, using interior lines to defeat two converging French armies in succession.
key_figures: Charles de Rohan, prince de Soubise (French commander)

sources.name: Westphalen, Geschichte der Feldzüge des Herzogs Ferdinand von Braunschweig-Lüneburg (1859–1872)
Contemporary dispatches in London Gazette, 1761

events.certainty_note: Well-documented European battle with extensive official correspondence surviving.

events.title: Spain enters the Seven Years' War (Third Family Compact)
events.description: On 15 August 1761, France and Spain signed the Third Family Compact (Pacte de Famille), a military alliance between the Bourbon monarchies. Spain's King Charles III, alarmed by British naval dominance and colonial expansion, agreed to enter the war against Britain. The pact was discovered by British intelligence, leading Britain to preemptively declare war on Spain in January 1762. Spain's entry widened the conflict but ultimately proved disastrous — Britain captured Havana and Manila in 1762, and Spain ceded Florida at the 1763 peace.
key_figures: Étienne François, duc de Choiseul (French foreign minister)
Marqués de Grimaldi (Spanish ambassador to France)

sources.name: Treaty text preserved in Spanish and French diplomatic archives
William Coxe, Memoirs of the Kings of Spain of the House of Bourbon (1813)

events.certainty_note: The treaty survives in original and the date is undisputed.

events.title: British capture of Dominica
events.description: In June 1761, a British expedition under Lord Rollo and Sir James Douglas captured the French-held island of Dominica with minimal resistance. The operation was part of Britain's systematic campaign to seize French Caribbean sugar colonies during the Seven Years' War. Dominica was formally ceded to Britain at the 1763 Treaty of Paris.
key_figures: Andrew Rollo, 5th Baron Rollo (British army commander)

sources.name: Admiralty records, The National Archives (Kew), ADM series
Richard Pares, War and Trade in the West Indies 1739–1763 (1936)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British naval and military records.

events.title: George III marries Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz
events.description: On 8 September 1761, King George III married Princess Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz at the Chapel Royal, St. James's Palace. The marriage was arranged rapidly — Charlotte arrived in England only on the day before the wedding. The couple would have fifteen children, producing a dynasty that shaped British monarchy into the nineteenth century. Charlotte became a notable patron of the arts and botany, and Kew Gardens flourished under royal patronage during her years as queen consort.

sources.name: Court records and London Gazette, September 1761
John Brooke, King George III (1972)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in contemporary newspapers, court records, and official gazettes.

events.title: Hyder Ali seizes power in Mysore
events.description: By 1761, Hyder Ali, a military commander of non-royal origin, had effectively completed his takeover of the Kingdom of Mysore, reducing the Wodeyar dynasty to puppet rulers. He modernized the Mysorean army along European lines, introduced rocket artillery, and began expanding his territory across southern India. His rise created a formidable rival to both the Marathas and the British East India Company, leading to four Anglo-Mysore Wars over the following decades.
key_figures: Hyder Ali (de facto ruler of Mysore)
Krishnaraja Wodeyar II (nominal king of Mysore)

sources.name: Kirmani, History of Hydur Naik (Persian chronicle, later translated)
M.H. Gopal, Tipu Sultan's Mysore (1971)

events.certainty_note: The exact timeline of Hyder Ali's consolidation of power is somewhat fluid; 1761 is the conventional date for his effective control, though the process was gradual from the mid-1750s.

events.title: Transit of Venus observed by Mikhail Lomonosov
events.description: On 6 June 1761, the transit of Venus across the Sun was observed worldwide — a major international scientific event coordinated across dozens of stations from Siberia to the Cape of Good Hope to Newfoundland. The Russian polymath Mikhail Lomonosov, observing from St. Petersburg, correctly inferred from the optical effects at the planet's limb that Venus possesses an atmosphere. The transit observations were intended to calculate the solar parallax and thus the Earth-Sun distance, though atmospheric distortion ('the black drop effect') limited the precision of results.
key_figures: Nevil Maskelyne (British astronomer, observed from St. Helena)
Alexandre Guy Pingré (French astronomer, observed from Rodrigues Island)
Charles Mason and Jeremiah Dixon (observed from the Cape of Good Hope)

sources.name: Lomonosov, 'On the phenomenon of Venus on the Sun observed' (1761, published in Russian and German)
Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society, 1761–1762
Andrea Wulf, Chasing Venus: The Race to Measure the Heavens (2012)

events.certainty_note: The transit date is astronomically fixed. Lomonosov's atmospheric inference is documented in his published paper, though its recognition by Western European astronomers came much later.
cross_references: 1769-001 (Second transit of Venus observation, Captain Cook's voyage)

events.title: Bridgewater Canal extended to Manchester
events.description: In 1761, the Bridgewater Canal, engineered by James Brindley and commissioned by Francis Egerton, 3rd Duke of Bridgewater, was extended to reach Manchester. The canal, which carried coal from the Duke's mines at Worsley, halved the price of coal in Manchester and demonstrated the commercial viability of artificial inland waterways in Britain. It catalyzed a canal-building boom ('Canal Mania') that transformed British internal transport infrastructure and was a key enabler of the early Industrial Revolution.

sources.name: Contemporary newspaper reports and estate records
Hugh Malet, Bridgewater: The Canal Duke (1977)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in contemporary sources. The canal's opening dates are established from estate and newspaper records.

events.title: Rousseau publishes Julie, ou la nouvelle Héloïse
events.description: Jean-Jacques Rousseau's epistolary novel Julie, ou la nouvelle Héloïse was published in January 1761 and became one of the best-selling books of the eighteenth century. The novel's themes of passionate love, virtue, natural sentiment, and the corrupting influence of society crystallized key Romantic and pre-Romantic ideas. It profoundly influenced European literary culture and helped establish the novel of sensibility as a dominant genre, anticipating the Romantic movement by decades.

sources.name: Julie, ou la nouvelle Héloïse (1761, first edition published by Marc-Michel Rey, Amsterdam)
Robert Darnton, 'Readers respond to Rousseau' in The Great Cat Massacre (1984)

events.certainty_note: Publication date is well established from publisher's records and contemporary correspondence.
cross_references: 1762-003 (Rousseau publishes The Social Contract and Émile)

events.title: John Harrison completes the H4 marine chronometer sea trial
events.description: In 1761, John Harrison's fourth marine chronometer (H4) was tested on a voyage to Jamaica aboard HMS Deptford, with Harrison's son William accompanying the watch. The chronometer lost only 5.1 seconds over 81 days at sea, far exceeding the accuracy requirements of the Board of Longitude's prize. This demonstrated that longitude could be reliably determined at sea using a precision timepiece, revolutionizing oceanic navigation. However, the Board was reluctant to award the full prize, and Harrison spent years fighting for recognition.
key_figures: William Harrison (his son, accompanied the trial voyage)
Nevil Maskelyne (Astronomer Royal, later an antagonist to Harrison's claim)

sources.name: Board of Longitude records, Cambridge University Library (RGO 14 series)
Dava Sobel, Longitude (1995)

events.certainty_note: The trial voyage and its results are documented in Board of Longitude records and ship's logs.

events.title: Collapse of Maratha political unity after Panipat
events.description: The catastrophic defeat at Panipat killed Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao's son Vishwasrao and cousin Sadashivrao Bhau, along with tens of thousands of Maratha soldiers. The Peshwa himself, shattered by the news, died on 23 June 1761 at Parvati near Pune. His death accelerated the devolution of the Maratha Confederacy into a collection of semi-independent states — the Scindias, Holkars, Gaekwads, and Bhonsles — each pursuing separate policies. This fragmentation was a critical precondition for British supremacy in India.

sources.name: Marathi-language Peshwa Daftar (administrative records)
S.G. Sardesai, New History of the Marathas (1946–1948)

events.certainty_note: The Peshwa's death date and the political consequences are well attested in Maratha administrative records.

events.title: Russian forces besiege and take Kolberg (Kołobrzeg)
events.description: After multiple failed attempts in earlier years, Russian forces under General Pyotr Rumyantsev finally captured the Prussian fortress of Kolberg in Pomerania on 16 December 1761, after a prolonged siege. By late 1761, Frederick the Great's Prussia was in its most desperate strategic position of the Seven Years' War — exhausted financially, losing territory, and facing encirclement. Only the death of Empress Elizabeth of Russia in January 1762 (the 'Miracle of the House of Brandenburg') would save Prussia from potential destruction.
key_figures: Frederick II of Prussia (King of Prussia)

sources.name: Russian military archives and campaign records
Christopher Duffy, Frederick the Great: A Military Life (1985)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented siege with clear dates from multiple military records.
cross_references: 1762-004 (Death of Empress Elizabeth, Russia withdraws from war)

events.title: British expeditionary force captures Belle Île
events.description: In April–June 1761, a British amphibious force of about 8,000 troops under Major General Studholme Hodgson captured the French island of Belle Île off the coast of Brittany, after an initial repulse and a second landing. Britain held the island as a bargaining chip in peace negotiations, eventually exchanging it for Minorca at the 1763 Treaty of Paris.

sources.name: London Gazette dispatches, 1761
Julian Corbett, England in the Seven Years' War (1907)

events.certainty_note: Documented in British naval and military dispatches.

events.title: Joseph Black discovers latent heat
events.description: In 1761, the Scottish chemist and physician Joseph Black conducted experiments at the University of Glasgow demonstrating that heat could be absorbed by a substance during a phase change (e.g., melting ice) without a change in temperature — the concept of latent heat. Though not published formally until much later, Black lectured on his findings from 1761 onward. His work was foundational for thermodynamics and directly influenced James Watt's improvements to the steam engine, as Watt was an instrument maker at Glasgow during this period.
key_figures: James Watt (instrument maker, influenced by Black)

sources.name: Black's unpublished lecture notes (University of Glasgow archives)
John Robison (ed.), Lectures on the Elements of Chemistry by Joseph Black (1803, posthumous)

events.certainty_note: Black's experiments are generally dated to 1761 based on lecture records, but the exact chronology is debated since he did not publish formally. Some scholars place key experiments in 1760 or 1762.
cross_references: 1769-002 (Watt patents the separate condenser steam engine)

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaigns in Xinjiang consolidation
events.region: East Asia — Qing China / Central Asia
events.description: By 1761, the Qing dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor was consolidating its conquest of Xinjiang (the 'New Frontier'), completed militarily in 1759 with the defeat of the Dzungar Khanate and the suppression of Khoja rebellions. The administration of the new territories was being organized under military governors (jiangjun), and Qing garrisons were established across the Tarim Basin and Ili River valley. The conquest roughly doubled the geographic extent of the Qing Empire and eliminated the last major Central Asian nomadic threat to China.
key_figures: Agūi (Manchu official involved in frontier administration)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty)
Peter Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia (2005)

events.certainty_note: Qing administrative records for this period are extensive and detailed.
cross_references: 1759-002 (Final Qing conquest of Dzungaria and Altishahr)

events.title: Cherokee War continues in the American Southeast
events.description: The Anglo-Cherokee War (1758–1761) continued with a major British punitive expedition in the summer of 1761. Lieutenant Colonel James Grant led approximately 2,600 troops into Cherokee territory in the southern Appalachians, systematically destroying fifteen Middle Cherokee towns, burning crops, and driving inhabitants into the mountains. The devastation forced the Cherokee to negotiate peace, concluded by treaty in December 1761. The war illustrated both the volatility of British-Native alliances and the colonial strategy of agricultural destruction.

sources.name: South Carolina Gazette reports, 1761
John Oliphant, Peace and War on the Anglo-Cherokee Frontier 1756–63 (2001)

events.certainty_note: Colonial records, military dispatches, and newspaper accounts document the campaign in detail.

events.title: Leopold Auenbrugger publishes Inventum Novum on percussion diagnosis
events.description: In 1761, Viennese physician Leopold Auenbrugger published Inventum Novum ex Percussione Thoracis Humani, describing his technique of chest percussion — tapping the patient's torso and interpreting the resulting sounds to diagnose underlying conditions such as fluid in the chest cavity. Though initially neglected, the technique was later championed by Napoleon's physician Corvisart and became a foundational diagnostic method in clinical medicine, still taught and used today.

sources.name: Auenbrugger, Inventum Novum ex Percussione Thoracis Humani (1761, Latin text)
Roy Porter, The Greatest Benefit to Mankind: A Medical History of Humanity (1997)

events.certainty_note: The publication date is established from the original printed edition.

events.title: Haider Ali's invasion of Malabar
events.description: Having consolidated power in Mysore, Hyder Ali launched an invasion of the Malabar Coast (modern Kerala) in 1761, conquering the Zamorin of Calicut's territories and several smaller Nair kingdoms. Hyder Ali imposed his authority over the spice-producing coastal regions, bringing them under Mysorean control. This expansion brought Mysore into direct competition with the British East India Company's trade interests on the western coast and laid groundwork for future conflicts.

sources.name: K.M. Panikkar, Malabar and the Portuguese (1929) and related regional histories
British East India Company correspondence, Tellicherry factory records

events.certainty_note: The chronology of Hyder Ali's Malabar campaigns is approximate, spanning 1761–1763 depending on the source. The campaign beginning is generally placed in 1761.

events.title: William Pitt the Elder resigns as Secretary of State
events.description: On 5 October 1761, William Pitt the Elder resigned as Secretary of State after his cabinet colleagues refused to support a preemptive war against Spain. Pitt had been the driving force behind Britain's aggressive prosecution of the Seven Years' War and was widely regarded as the architect of British victories across the globe. His departure signaled a shift toward peace negotiations under Lord Bute's growing influence with George III, though Spain's subsequent entry into the war vindicated Pitt's strategic warnings.
key_figures: William Pitt the Elder (Secretary of State)
John Stuart, 3rd Earl of Bute (favorite and incoming chief minister)

sources.name: Parliamentary records and contemporary correspondence
Marie Peters, Pitt and Popularity (1998)

events.certainty_note: Pitt's resignation and its circumstances are extensively documented in political correspondence and parliamentary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The Third Battle of Panipat's casualty figures are highly variable across sources, ranging from 30,000 to over 100,000, and modern scholarship (e.g., Sarkar, Fall of the Mughal Empire) cautions against accepting the highest figures uncritically, as they may include camp followers and are drawn from partisan accounts on both sides. The claim that Panipat alone caused the decline of the Maratha Confederacy is complicated by the fact that under Madhavrao I (Peshwa from 1761), the Marathas staged a significant military and political recovery in the 1760s; the fragmentation was more gradual than the 'catastrophic single blow' narrative suggests. Regarding Joseph Black's latent heat experiments, the dating to 1761 specifically is conventional but imprecise — Black's own records are ambiguous and some historians (e.g., Henry Guerlac) have argued the key insights emerged over 1759–1762. Lomonosov's inference of a Venusian atmosphere in 1761, while now credited, was essentially unknown outside Russia for over a century and its 'significance' is partly retrospective — contemporary Western astronomers independently debated the issue without reference to his work.
historiographic_note: The year 1761 is richly documented from European, South Asian, and Qing Chinese sources. European records are dominated by Seven Years' War military dispatches, diplomatic correspondence, and parliamentary proceedings — primarily from British, French, Prussian, and Russian state archives. South Asian sources include Marathi-language Peshwa administrative records (Peshwa Daftar), Persian-language court chronicles from both the Durrani and Mughal courts, and British East India Company correspondence. Qing records are drawn from the Veritable Records and palace memorials. The overwhelming bias is toward state-sponsored military and diplomatic history; economic life, demographic movements, and the experiences of ordinary people are poorly represented. Sub-Saharan African history for this period relies heavily on European trading-post records and later oral traditions, creating significant gaps. Indigenous American perspectives on events like the Cherokee War are filtered almost entirely through colonial sources.

graph_edges.note: Both the Panipat catastrophe and Plassey were pivotal moments in the multi-way struggle for post-Mughal supremacy in India
The Panipat defeat directly caused the death of the Peshwa and fragmentation of the Maratha Confederacy
Fall of Pondicherry finalized British supremacy in India, confirmed at the Treaty of Paris
Spain's entry into the war led to British campaigns against Spanish colonies in 1762
The 1761 transit of Venus observations informed planning for the more successful 1769 transit expedition
The Manchester extension built on the initial Worsley-to-Manchester canal section opened in 1759
Harrison's chronometer was built in direct response to the Longitude Act's prize
Prussia's desperate position after Kolberg's fall makes the Miracle of the House of Brandenburg (Elizabeth's death) historically consequential
Black's latent heat theory directly informed Watt's separate condenser design
Pitt's resignation shifted British war policy toward negotiation and the eventual peace treaty

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration

era_context: The mid-18th century is defined by the Seven Years' War (1756–1763), a truly global conflict involving European powers and their colonial empires across four continents. In East Asia, the Qing Dynasty under the Qianlong Emperor is at its territorial zenith. The European Enlightenment is reshaping intellectual and political culture, while the Atlantic slave trade is at peak volume. In India, the Mughal Empire has fragmented into successor states contested by the Marathas, regional nawabs, and European trading companies.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal trading posts
Interior Southeast Asia
Aboriginal Australia
Pacific Islands
Interior South America

events.title: Battle of Wandiwash
events.description: On 22 January 1760, British forces under Sir Eyre Coote decisively defeated the French under the Marquis de Bussy-Castelnau at Wandiwash (Vandavasi) in the Carnatic. The French commander Thomas Arthur, Comte de Lally, had besieged the fort, but Coote's relief force routed the French army and captured Bussy. This battle effectively ended French ambitions for territorial dominance in India and paved the way for British supremacy on the subcontinent. The French were subsequently confined to a handful of trading posts.
key_figures: Comte de Lally (French commander-in-chief in India)
Marquis de Bussy-Castelnau (French field commander, captured)

sources.name: British East India Company records and dispatches
G.B. Malleson, History of the French in India (1868)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented engagement with extensive British and French military records.

events.title: Death of George II and accession of George III
events.description: George II died on 25 October 1760 at Kensington Palace, the last British monarch born outside Great Britain. His grandson George III succeeded him at age 22, beginning a reign that would last until 1820 and encompass the loss of the American colonies, the Napoleonic Wars, and profound constitutional change. George III's early reign was marked by his desire to break the Whig oligarchy and assert greater royal influence over ministerial appointments, leading to political instability in the 1760s.
key_figures: George II (King of Great Britain, deceased)
George III (new King of Great Britain)

sources.name: Horace Walpole, Memoirs of the Reign of King George the Second
London Gazette, October 1760

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated from court records and contemporary press.

events.title: Fall of Montreal and British conquest of New France
events.description: On 8 September 1760, the Marquis de Vaudreuil, Governor of New France, surrendered Montreal to British forces under General Jeffery Amherst, completing the British conquest of Canada. Three British armies had converged on Montreal from Quebec, Lake Champlain, and Lake Ontario, leaving the French no avenue of retreat or resupply. The capitulation effectively ended French sovereignty in mainland North America, a transfer formalized by the Treaty of Paris in 1763. The terms granted French Canadians the right to practice Catholicism and retain property.
key_figures: Jeffery Amherst (British commander-in-chief in North America)
Marquis de Vaudreuil (Governor-General of New France)

sources.name: Articles of Capitulation of Montreal, 8 September 1760
Francis Parkman, Montcalm and Wolfe (1884)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in British and French military and diplomatic archives.

events.title: Battle of Liegnitz
events.description: On 15 August 1760, Frederick the Great of Prussia surprised and defeated an Austrian army under General Ernst von Laudon at Liegnitz (Legnica) in Silesia. Laudon had planned a night attack, but Frederick, having intercepted Austrian communications, repositioned his forces and struck first at dawn. The Prussians inflicted roughly 10,000 casualties and captured 82 guns. The victory prevented the Austrians from crushing Frederick's outnumbered army and demonstrated his continued tactical brilliance despite Prussia's increasingly desperate strategic position in the Seven Years' War.
key_figures: Frederick II of Prussia (King and commander)
Ernst Gideon von Laudon (Austrian field commander)

sources.name: Frederick the Great, correspondence and military dispatches
Christopher Duffy, Frederick the Great: A Military Life (1985)

events.certainty_note: Detailed in Prussian and Austrian military records.

events.title: Battle of Torgau
events.description: On 3 November 1760, Frederick the Great won the last major pitched battle of the Seven Years' War in Europe at Torgau, defeating an entrenched Austrian army under Field Marshal Leopold von Daun. It was one of the bloodiest battles of the war, with combined casualties exceeding 30,000. Frederick himself was struck by a spent musket ball and briefly incapacitated. The Prussian victory, achieved largely by Hans Joachim von Zieten's flanking attack, preserved Saxony for Prussia but left Frederick's army too weakened for further offensive operations.
key_figures: Frederick II of Prussia (King and commander)
Hans Joachim von Zieten (Prussian cavalry general)

sources.name: Austrian and Prussian regimental histories and campaign dispatches
Franz A.J. Szabo, The Seven Years War in Europe, 1756–1763 (2008)

events.certainty_note: Precisely documented in military archives of multiple combatant nations.

events.title: Russian forces briefly occupy Berlin
events.description: In October 1760, a combined Russian and Austrian raiding force under General Gottlob Heinrich Totleben occupied Berlin for several days (9–12 October), extracting a heavy contribution of 1.5 million thalers from the city. The occupation, though brief and without lasting strategic effect, humiliated Prussia and underscored the precariousness of Frederick's position. The raiders withdrew upon learning of Frederick's approach. The event demonstrated the overextension of Prussian defenses across multiple fronts.
key_figures: Friedrich Wilhelm von Seydlitz (Prussian cavalry commander, absent)

sources.name: Berlin city council records of contributions paid
Christopher Duffy, Russia's Military Way to the West (1981)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in both Russian and Prussian sources.

events.title: Siege and fall of Pondicherry begins
events.description: Following the French defeat at Wandiwash, British forces under Sir Eyre Coote began the siege of Pondicherry (Puducherry), the principal French settlement in India, in September 1760. The siege would last until January 1761, when the starving garrison surrendered. The fall of Pondicherry marked the effective end of French India as a political and military entity, reducing French presence to a few minor comptoirs returned after the war. This was the Indian theatre's decisive conclusion of the Seven Years' War.

sources.name: British East India Company military records
S.P. Sen, The French in India (1947)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in Company and Crown records.

events.title: Qianlong Emperor's campaigns in Xinjiang consolidation
events.description: By 1760, the Qianlong Emperor had completed the Qing conquest of the Dzungar Khanate and the Tarim Basin oases, incorporating the vast region of Xinjiang ('New Frontier') into the empire. The final pacification of resistance in the Altishahr (southern Xinjiang) region was accomplished with the defeat of the Khoja brothers in 1759, and 1760 saw the establishment of administrative structures including military governors (jiangjun) at Ili. This expansion brought the Qing Empire to its maximum territorial extent, roughly 13 million square kilometers.
key_figures: Zhaohui (Qing general who completed the Xinjiang campaigns)

sources.name: Qing Shilu (Veritable Records of the Qing Dynasty), Qianlong reign
Peter C. Perdue, China Marches West: The Qing Conquest of Central Eurasia (2005)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Qing imperial records and Central Asian sources. The administrative establishment in 1760 is precisely dated.

events.title: Cherokee War escalates on the Carolina frontier
events.description: The Anglo-Cherokee War (1758–1761) escalated sharply in 1760. After the killing of Cherokee hostages at Fort Prince George in February, Cherokee warriors besieged Fort Loudoun in eastern Tennessee. A relief expedition under Colonel Archibald Montgomery marched into Cherokee territory, burned Lower Towns, but was ambushed and repulsed at the Battle of Echoee (27 June). Fort Loudoun's garrison surrendered on 10 August and was attacked the next day in violation of the surrender terms, with about two dozen soldiers killed. The conflict revealed the limits of British frontier power even as they conquered Canada.
key_figures: Captain Paul Demere (Fort Loudoun commander, killed)

sources.name: South Carolina Gazette reports, 1760
Tom Hatley, The Dividing Paths: Cherokees and South Carolinians through the Era of Revolution (1993)

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in colonial records, military dispatches, and later Cherokee oral tradition.

events.title: Maratha Panipat campaign begins
events.description: In 1760, the Maratha Confederacy under Sadashivrao Bhau launched a major expedition northward to confront Ahmad Shah Durrani's Afghan forces and their Indian allies. The Marathas recaptured Delhi and then advanced toward the Punjab, but logistical overextension and failure to secure local alliances placed them in an increasingly vulnerable position. By late 1760, both armies were encamped near Panipat, setting the stage for the devastating Third Battle of Panipat in January 1761, which would shatter Maratha power in North India for a generation.
key_figures: Balaji Baji Rao (Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy)

sources.name: Marathi bakhar (chronicle) traditions, including the Panipat Bakhar
T.S. Shejwalkar, Panipat 1761 (1946)

events.certainty_note: The campaign's progression is well-attested in multiple Indian, Afghan, and European sources, though exact dates of some engagements are debated.

events.title: Josiah Wedgwood opens the Ivy House pottery works
events.description: In 1760, Josiah Wedgwood established his own pottery manufacturing business at the Ivy House Works in Burslem, Staffordshire, after years as a journeyman and partner. Wedgwood would become the most influential potter of the Industrial Revolution, pioneering the application of scientific method to ceramics, industrial division of labor, and modern marketing techniques including money-back guarantees and illustrated catalogues. His enterprise exemplified the transformation of craft production into industrial manufacturing that was beginning to reshape the English Midlands.

sources.name: Wedgwood business correspondence and records
Jenny Uglow, The Lunar Men (2002)

events.certainty_note: Dated from Wedgwood's business records, though exact month is uncertain.

events.title: Great fire destroys much of Boston
events.description: On 20 March 1760, a major fire broke out in Boston, destroying approximately 349 buildings — roughly one-tenth of the town — and leaving over 1,000 people homeless. The fire devastated the commercial district near the wharves. It was one of the worst urban fires in colonial American history and prompted significant rebuilding efforts as well as early discussion of fire codes and building regulations. The fire struck a town already strained by wartime costs and military impressment.

sources.name: Boston Gazette reports, March 1760
Samuel Drake, The History and Antiquities of Boston (1856)

events.certainty_note: Documented in contemporary newspapers and town records.

events.title: Bridgewater Canal opens near Manchester
events.description: In 1760, construction on the Bridgewater Canal, commissioned by the Duke of Bridgewater and engineered by James Brindley, was well advanced, with the first section opening in July 1761. The canal was designed to transport coal from the Duke's mines at Worsley to Manchester. While the formal opening came in 1761, the construction during 1760 — including the innovative Barton Aqueduct carrying the canal over the River Irwell — captured public imagination and demonstrated the feasibility of artificial waterways for industrial transport. It catalyzed the British canal mania of subsequent decades.
key_figures: John Gilbert (mining agent and canal supervisor)

sources.name: Parliamentary records for the Bridgewater Canal Act (1759)
Hugh Malet, Bridgewater: The Canal Duke (1977)

events.certainty_note: Construction timeline documented in parliamentary records, estate papers, and contemporary accounts. The canal's opening is properly dated to 1761; 1760 represents the major construction phase.

events.title: Macpherson publishes Fragments of Ancient Poetry (Ossian)
events.description: In June 1760, James Macpherson published Fragments of Ancient Poetry, Collected in the Highlands of Scotland, purportedly translations of ancient Gaelic poems by the 3rd-century bard Ossian. The work caused a literary sensation across Europe and became foundational to the Romantic movement, influencing Goethe, Napoleon, and the rise of Celtic cultural nationalism. The authenticity of the poems was immediately and persistently disputed — Samuel Johnson was a prominent skeptic — and modern scholarship regards them as largely Macpherson's own composition, loosely based on genuine Gaelic oral tradition.
key_figures: Hugh Blair (Edinburgh professor who championed the work)

sources.name: James Macpherson, Fragments of Ancient Poetry (1760, Edinburgh)
